teens.explanation: He that putteth not out his money to usury, nor taketh reward against the innocent (Psalm 15:5).

teens.question: What does the God-fearer do regarding vile persons versus those who fear God?
teens.option_a: He despises a vile person and honours them that fear the LORD
teens.option_c: He avoids vile persons only in the temple
teens.option_d: He rebukes all evil doers publicly
teens.explanation: In whose eyes a vile person is contemned; but he honoureth them that fear the LORD (Psalm 15:4).

teens.question: Who does the upright man honour and who does he despise?
teens.option_a: He honours them that fear the LORD and despises a vile person
teens.option_b: He honours the king and despises the poor
teens.option_c: He honours the priests and despises sinners
teens.option_d: He honours the wise and despises fools
teens.explanation: In whose eyes a vile person is contemned; but he honoureth them that fear the LORD (Psalm 15:4).

teens.question: What promise does Psalm 15 end with for the person who does these things?
teens.option_c: He shall rule over the nations
teens.explanation: He that doeth these things shall never be moved (Psalm 15:5).

adults.question: How does Psalm 15 function as a "Torah liturgy" in its original temple context?
adults.option_b: It was likely used as an entrance liturgy — a question and answer about the moral qualifications required to approach God in worship
adults.option_c: It was sung during harvest festivals
adults.option_d: It was a psalm for private devotion only
adults.explanation: Psalm 15 is an entrance liturgy or "Torah liturgy" — the question in verse 1 represents the entering worshipper, and verses 2-5 give the moral answer to who may approach God.

adults.question: How do the ethical requirements of Psalm 15 relate to the Ten Commandments?
adults.option_b: The ethical qualities of Psalm 15 are largely a positive restatement of the second table of the Ten Commandments applied to social life
adults.option_c: Psalm 15 replaces the Ten Commandments
adults.option_d: The Ten Commandments are not related to Psalm 15
adults.explanation: The moral portrait of Psalm 15 — truth, justice, no slander, no bribery — reflects the second table of the Decalogue applied to neighbour relationships.

adults.question: What does "backbiteth not with his tongue" in Psalm 15:3 guard against?
adults.option_b: Secret slander and gossip that undermines a person's reputation in their absence
adults.option_c: Public insult
adults.option_d: Speaking too much
adults.explanation: He that backbiteth not with his tongue, nor doeth evil to his neighbour (Psalm 15:3) — backbiting is secret slander, speaking evil of someone behind their back.

adults.question: What is the moral significance of the clause "he that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not" in Psalm 15:4?
adults.option_b: Integrity requires keeping commitments even when it is costly to do so
adults.option_c: Oaths are only binding if witnessed
adults.option_d: Promises should be renegotiated when circumstances change
adults.explanation: He that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not (Psalm 15:4) — the mark of integrity is keeping one's word even at personal cost.

adults.question: What is the prohibition against usury in Psalm 15:5 and what is its social context?
adults.option_a: All interest on any money is forbidden
adults.option_b: Charging interest on loans to the poor was forbidden in the Mosaic law (Exodus 22:25) — Psalm 15:5 applies this to the character of the righteous
adults.option_c: Only loans to foreigners could bear interest
adults.option_d: Banking was entirely forbidden in Israel
adults.explanation: He that putteth not out his money to usury (Psalm 15:5) — usury (interest on loans to poor Israelites) was forbidden in Exodus 22:25, reflecting God's concern for economic justice.

adults.question: How does Psalm 15 answer the question of access to God differently from ritual purity laws?
adults.option_a: It emphasises ritual purity over ethics
adults.option_b: It shifts the focus entirely to moral and ethical integrity — it is character, not ritual status, that defines who may approach God
adults.option_c: It requires sacrifices before entry
adults.option_d: It requires priestly lineage
adults.explanation: Psalm 15 answers the question of access to God entirely in ethical, not ritual, terms — integrity, truth, justice — a radical emphasis on moral character.

adults.question: What does "nor taketh reward against the innocent" in Psalm 15:5 condemn?
adults.option_b: Taking bribes to pervert justice against an innocent person
adults.option_c: Accepting gifts of any kind
adults.option_d: Charging fees for teaching the law
adults.explanation: Nor taketh reward against the innocent (Psalm 15:5) — this condemns bribery in the judicial system, a major concern of the prophets (cf. Isaiah 1:23, Amos 5:12).

adults.question: What does the conclusion "he shall never be moved" in Psalm 15:5 promise, and what does it echo throughout the Psalter?
adults.option_b: Unshakeable stability and security in God — echoing the stability of the righteous man of Psalm 1 and the trust-theme throughout the Psalter
adults.option_c: Physical immortality
adults.option_d: Permanent residence in Jerusalem
adults.explanation: He that doeth these things shall never be moved (Psalm 15:5) — the "not moved" promise echoes Psalm 1:3 and recurs throughout the Psalter.

kids.question: Where does David say he has a goodly heritage in Psalm 16?
kids.option_a: The lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places
kids.option_d: In the house of the LORD
kids.explanation: The lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places; yea, I have a goodly heritage (Psalm 16:6).

kids.question: What does David say God will not leave his soul in?
kids.explanation: For thou wilt not leave my soul in hell; neither wilt thou suffer thine Holy One to see corruption (Psalm 16:10).

kids.question: What does David say is at God's right hand?
kids.explanation: At thy right hand there are pleasures for evermore (Psalm 16:11).

teens.question: What does David say about the LORD being always before him in Psalm 16:8?
teens.explanation: I have set the LORD always before me: because he is at my right hand, I shall not be moved (Psalm 16:8).

teens.question: What does David's heart do and what is glad according to Psalm 16:9?
teens.option_a: My heart is glad, and my glory rejoiceth: my flesh also shall rest in hope
teens.option_d: His heart leaps for joy in battle
teens.explanation: Therefore my heart is glad, and my glory rejoiceth: my flesh also shall rest in hope (Psalm 16:9).

teens.question: What does David call the LORD in Psalm 16:2?
teens.option_a: O my soul, thou hast said, Thou art my Lord
teens.explanation: O my soul, thou hast said unto the LORD, Thou art my Lord: my goodness extendeth not to thee (Psalm 16:2).

teens.question: What is the path of life associated with in Psalm 16:11?
teens.option_a: In thy presence is fulness of joy
teens.option_b: In the house of the LORD
teens.option_c: In the keeping of the law
teens.explanation: Thou wilt shew me the path of life: in thy presence is fulness of joy (Psalm 16:11).

teens.question: What does David say about those who run after other gods in Psalm 16:4?
teens.explanation: Their sorrows shall be multiplied that hasten after another god (Psalm 16:4).

adults.question: In what two New Testament passages is Psalm 16:8-11 quoted, and by whom?
adults.option_b: By Peter in Acts 2:25-28 and Paul in Acts 13:35 — both applying it to the resurrection of Christ
adults.option_c: By Luke and Mark
adults.option_d: By Paul in Romans and Hebrews
adults.explanation: Peter quotes Psalm 16:8-11 in Acts 2:25-28 applying it to Christ's resurrection; Paul quotes verse 10 in Acts 13:35 as evidence of Christ's resurrection.

adults.question: What is the key argument Peter makes in Acts 2 about Psalm 16:10?
adults.option_a: David wrote it about himself and was fulfilled in his own lifetime
adults.option_b: David was a prophet and spoke of Christ's resurrection — David himself saw corruption, but Christ did not
adults.option_c: The psalm does not refer to the resurrection
adults.option_d: David was predicting his own escape from death
adults.explanation: Peter argues in Acts 2:29-31 that David died and saw corruption, so Psalm 16:10 — "neither wilt thou suffer thine Holy One to see corruption" — must refer to Christ.

adults.question: What is the title "Michtam of David" in the superscription thought to mean?
adults.option_b: Possibly "golden psalm" or a psalm of special value — the exact meaning is uncertain
adults.option_c: A war song
adults.option_d: A lament psalm
adults.explanation: Michtam (Psalm 16 superscription) appears in six psalm titles (16, 56-60) and may mean "golden" or a psalm of special worth — the exact meaning is debated.

adults.question: What is David's attitude toward the pagan sacrificial system in Psalm 16:4?
adults.option_b: He refuses to participate in their drink offerings of blood or take their names upon his lips
adults.option_c: He mildly disapproves
adults.option_d: He offers critique only of foreign nations' practices
adults.explanation: Their drink offerings of blood will I not offer, nor take up their names into my lips (Psalm 16:4) — David's complete separation from pagan worship.

adults.question: What do "the lot" and "the lines" in Psalm 16:5-6 represent in the context of the land covenant?
adults.option_b: God as David's divinely appointed inheritance and portion — using the language of the land allotment to Israel
adults.option_c: A specific piece of land David owned
adults.option_d: The royal treasury
adults.explanation: The LORD is the portion of mine inheritance and of my cup: thou maintainest my lot. The lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places (Psalm 16:5-6) — land allotment language applied to God himself as portion.

adults.question: What does David mean by "my reins also instruct me in the night seasons" in Psalm 16:7?
adults.option_b: God's counsel penetrates to his deepest inner being, even in sleep — the reins (kidneys) represent the seat of conscience
adults.option_c: He reads the law at night
adults.option_d: He receives angelic visions
adults.explanation: My reins also instruct me in the night seasons (Psalm 16:7) — the reins (kidneys) in Hebrew thought were the seat of deep moral instruction and conscience.

adults.question: How does Psalm 16 bridge the individual experience of David and the corporate resurrection hope of Messiah?
adults.option_b: David's personal experience of trust in God becomes prophetically applied to the Messiah's death and resurrection by Peter and Paul
adults.option_c: It is only about Israel as a nation
adults.option_d: It has no prophetic dimension
adults.explanation: Psalm 16 begins as David's personal trust (vv.1-8), but Peter and Paul identify its ultimate fulfilment in Christ's resurrection — a typological bridge (Acts 2:25-28; 13:35).

adults.question: What is the climactic promise of Psalm 16:11?
adults.option_b: The path of life, fulness of joy in God's presence, and pleasures forevermore — the ultimate resolution of the soul's longing for God
adults.option_c: Promise of long earthly life
adults.option_d: Promise of victory over enemies
adults.explanation: Thou wilt shew me the path of life: in thy presence is fulness of joy; at thy right hand there are pleasures for evermore (Psalm 16:11).

kids.question: What does David ask God to keep him as in Psalm 17?
kids.explanation: Keep me as the apple of the eye, hide me under the shadow of thy wings (Psalm 17:8).

kids.question: What will David see when he wakes up?
kids.explanation: As for me, I will behold thy face in righteousness: I shall be satisfied, when I awake, with thy likeness (Psalm 17:15).

kids.question: Where does David ask God to hide him?
kids.option_a: Under the shadow of thy wings
kids.option_c: In the depths of the earth
kids.explanation: Hide me under the shadow of thy wings (Psalm 17:8).

teens.question: What does David ask God to hear in Psalm 17:1?
teens.option_a: A just cause, my cry, and my prayer that goeth not out of feigned lips
teens.explanation: Hear the right, O LORD, attend unto my cry, give ear unto my prayer, that goeth not out of feigned lips (Psalm 17:1).

teens.question: What test does David invite God to perform in Psalm 17:3?
teens.option_a: Thou hast proved mine heart; thou hast visited me in the night
teens.option_b: Examine my deeds and my words
teens.option_c: Look at my offerings and my sacrifices
teens.option_d: Count my enemies and my prayers
teens.explanation: Thou hast proved mine heart; thou hast visited me in the night; thou hast tried me, and shalt find nothing (Psalm 17:3).

teens.question: What do the enemies encircle David like in Psalm 17:12?
teens.option_a: A lion that is greedy of his prey
teens.explanation: Like as a lion that is greedy of his prey, and as it were a young lion lurking in secret places (Psalm 17:12).

teens.question: What do the wicked have in this life according to Psalm 17:14?
teens.option_a: Their portion in this life and whose belly thou fillest with thy hid treasure
teens.explanation: Men of the world, which have their portion in this life, and whose belly thou fillest with thy hid treasure (Psalm 17:14).

teens.question: What does David say his steps have done in Psalm 17:5?
teens.option_a: Held fast to God's paths and not slipped
teens.option_d: Brought him to the house of the LORD
teens.explanation: Hold up my goings in thy paths, that my footsteps slip not (Psalm 17:5).

adults.question: What is the title of Psalm 17 in the KJV, and what does "A Prayer of David" indicate?
adults.option_b: It is one of the few psalms explicitly titled "A Prayer" — indicating a direct petition to God rather than praise or meditation
adults.option_c: A Shiggaion — a wild ode
adults.option_d: A Maschil — an instructional poem
adults.explanation: Psalm 17 is titled "A Prayer of David" — one of five explicitly prayer-titled psalms (17, 86, 90, 102, 142), emphasizing direct petition.

adults.question: What does "that goeth not out of feigned lips" in Psalm 17:1 add to David's petition?
adults.option_a: That his prayer is poetically composed
adults.option_b: That his prayer is utterly sincere — not hypocritical or performed for human approval
adults.option_c: That he is speaking quietly
adults.option_d: That he wrote the prayer in advance
adults.explanation: Give ear unto my prayer, that goeth not out of feigned lips (Psalm 17:1) — David appeals to God on the basis of the utter sincerity of his prayer.

adults.question: What is the "apple of the eye" in Psalm 17:8 as a cultural and anatomical expression?
adults.option_b: The pupil — the most sensitive and protected part of the eye, used as a metaphor for that which is most precious and carefully guarded
adults.option_c: The eye of God himself
adults.option_d: A jewel worn over the eye
adults.explanation: Keep me as the apple of the eye (Psalm 17:8) — the "apple" (Hebrew: iyshon, little man) refers to the pupil, the most carefully protected part of the body.

adults.question: What does the "shadow of thy wings" imagery in Psalm 17:8 evoke?
adults.option_b: The protective wings of the cherubim over the ark and the mother bird sheltering her young — divine protection and tender care
adults.option_c: Storm clouds
adults.option_d: The wings of an eagle carrying Israel
adults.explanation: Hide me under the shadow of thy wings (Psalm 17:8) — evoking both the cherubim wings over the ark (Exodus 25:20) and the mother bird protecting her young (cf. Psalm 91:4).

adults.question: How does David's protestation of innocence in Psalm 17:3-5 relate to the legal context of the psalm?
adults.option_b: In the context of false accusations, David appeals to his proven integrity as the basis for God's vindication — it is a legal declaration of innocence before the divine judge
adults.option_c: It shows David was sinless
adults.option_d: It contradicts Psalm 51
adults.explanation: Thou hast proved mine heart...thou hast tried me, and shalt find nothing (Psalm 17:3) — a legal declaration of innocence before God as judge in the face of false accusation.

adults.question: What contrast does Psalm 17:14-15 draw between the destiny of the wicked and the righteous?
adults.option_a: The wicked suffer now and the righteous prosper now
adults.option_b: The wicked have their portion in this life; the righteous will be satisfied with God's likeness at the resurrection
adults.option_c: Both receive earthly rewards
adults.option_d: The righteous receive more earthly goods
adults.explanation: The wicked have their portion in this life (v.14), but David will be satisfied with thy likeness at the awakening (v.15) — a profound contrast of temporal versus eternal destiny.

adults.question: What does "I shall be satisfied, when I awake, with thy likeness" in Psalm 17:15 anticipate theologically?
adults.option_b: The resurrection — the ultimate satisfaction of seeing God's face and being transformed into his likeness
adults.option_c: A morning prayer
adults.option_d: Physical healing
adults.explanation: I shall be satisfied, when I awake, with thy likeness (Psalm 17:15) — widely understood as pointing to the resurrection and final vision of God (cf. 1 John 3:2).

adults.question: Why does David say "from men which are thy hand" in Psalm 17:14, and what is the theological implication?
adults.option_a: He is asking to be saved from angels
adults.option_b: Even the wicked enemies who threaten him are instruments in God's sovereign hand — so God can restrain them
adults.option_c: He is asking for deliverance from the king's soldiers
adults.option_d: He is asking God to use human helpers
adults.explanation: Deliver my soul from the wicked, which is thy sword: from men which are thy hand (Psalm 17:13-14) — even enemies are God's instruments, and God can withdraw their power.

kids.question: What does David call the LORD at the very start of Psalm 18?
kids.explanation: I will love thee, O LORD, my strength (Psalm 18:1).

kids.question: What does David say about God's way in Psalm 18:30?
kids.option_a: As for God, his way is perfect
kids.explanation: As for God, his way is perfect: the word of the LORD is tried (Psalm 18:30).

kids.question: What does David say God makes his feet like?
kids.explanation: He maketh my feet like hinds' feet, and setteth me upon my high places (Psalm 18:33).

teens.question: What is the parallel passage to Psalm 18 found elsewhere in the Bible?
teens.explanation: Psalm 18 is nearly identical to the song in 2 Samuel 22, which David sang after God delivered him from all his enemies.

teens.question: With what did God come down when He rescued David from great enemies in Psalm 18?
teens.option_a: Darkness, clouds, coals of fire, hailstones, and thunder
teens.option_b: A rushing wind and bright light
teens.option_d: Fire and a still small voice
teens.explanation: He bowed the heavens also, and came down: and darkness was under his feet...there went up a smoke out of his nostrils, and fire out of his mouth devoured (Psalm 18:9-12).

teens.question: How does David say God rewards the froward in Psalm 18:26?
teens.option_d: He turns them to his servants
teens.explanation: With the froward thou wilt shew thyself froward (Psalm 18:26).

teens.question: What six names does David give the LORD in Psalm 18:2?
teens.option_a: My rock, fortress, deliverer, God, strength, buckler
teens.option_b: My shepherd, light, salvation, king, hope, joy
teens.option_c: My fire, wind, thunder, rain, cloud, star
teens.option_d: My sword, shield, spear, arrow, bow, wall
teens.explanation: The LORD is my rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer; my God, my strength, in whom I will trust; my buckler, and the horn of my salvation, and my high tower (Psalm 18:2).

teens.question: What Messianic note does Psalm 18 close with regarding David's seed?
teens.option_a: He sheweth mercy to his anointed, to David, and to his seed for evermore
teens.option_b: The seed of David shall rule all nations
teens.option_c: The throne of David shall stand forever
teens.option_d: The descendants of David will build the temple
teens.explanation: He is the tower of salvation for his king: and sheweth mercy to his anointed, to David, and to his seed for evermore (Psalm 18:50).

adults.question: What is the historical context given in the superscription of Psalm 18?
adults.option_a: David's flight from Saul in the cave of Adullam
adults.option_b: The day the LORD delivered David from the hand of all his enemies and from the hand of Saul
adults.option_c: David's victory over the Philistines at the valley of Elah
adults.option_d: David's prayer upon his coronation as king
adults.explanation: The superscription states: "A Psalm of David...in the day that the LORD delivered him from the hand of all his enemies, and from the hand of Saul."

adults.question: What does "the cords of death compassed me" in Psalm 18:4-5 signify?
adults.option_b: The Hebrew word (hebel — cord/snare) suggests the inescapable grip of death surrounding David like hunting nets
adults.option_c: A rope used in torture
adults.option_d: Chains of imprisonment
adults.explanation: The sorrows of death compassed me...the snares of death prevented me (Psalm 18:4-5) — hebel (cords) and snares evoke the inescapable entrapment of approaching death.

adults.question: What literary device is used in Psalm 18:9-15 when God descends to rescue David?
adults.option_b: A theophany — a dramatic description of God's self-manifestation using storm imagery (darkness, fire, lightning, thunder) as in Sinai
adults.option_c: A dream vision
adults.option_d: A historical account
adults.explanation: Psalm 18:9-15 is a theophany — God's dramatic appearance using cosmic storm imagery echoing the Sinai theophany of Exodus 19.

adults.question: What does the phrase "with the pure thou wilt shew thyself pure" in Psalm 18:26 teach about God's moral responsiveness?
adults.option_b: God's dealings with people mirror their own moral disposition — the pure experience his purity, the froward experience his opposition
adults.option_c: God rewards only outward purity
adults.option_d: God changes his character based on human sin
adults.explanation: Psalm 18:25-26 teaches divine moral responsiveness — God relates to the merciful with mercy, the pure with purity, and the froward with opposition.

adults.question: What does "thou wilt save the afflicted people; but wilt bring down high looks" in Psalm 18:27 express?
adults.option_a: God favours the poor over the rich
adults.option_b: The consistent biblical pattern of divine reversal — the humble are exalted and the proud are brought low
adults.option_c: God despises the proud for their appearance
adults.option_d: The afflicted always deserve salvation
adults.explanation: Thou wilt save the afflicted people; but wilt bring down high looks (Psalm 18:27) — the reversal theme central to biblical theology (cf. Luke 1:51-53, James 4:6).

adults.question: What does Paul quote from Psalm 18:49 in Romans 15:9, and why?
adults.option_a: To prove David was a prophet
adults.option_b: To show that Gentile praise was prophesied in the Old Testament — David's praise among the nations foreshadows Gentile inclusion
adults.option_c: To prove the resurrection
adults.option_d: To establish David's authority
adults.explanation: Paul quotes Psalm 18:49 — "I will give praise unto thee among the Gentiles" — in Romans 15:9 as scriptural support for the Gentile mission.

adults.question: What does "enlarged my steps under me" in Psalm 18:36 mean?
adults.option_b: God expanded the secure footing available to David — preventing stumbling in dangerous terrain, metaphorically and literally
adults.option_c: God gave David more territory
adults.option_d: God provided David with better sandals
adults.explanation: Thou hast enlarged my steps under me, that my feet did not slip (Psalm 18:36) — enlarging one's steps means giving secure footing, preventing stumbling.

adults.question: How does Psalm 18 close with a Messianic note regarding the house of David?
adults.option_b: David declares God's great deliverance to his king and his seed for evermore — pointing to the Davidic covenant and its Messianic fulfilment
adults.option_c: With a praise for the nation of Israel
adults.option_d: With a prediction of the temple
adults.explanation: He is the tower of salvation for his king: and sheweth mercy to his anointed, to David, and to his seed for evermore (Psalm 18:50) — pointing to the Davidic covenant and its Messianic line.

kids.question: What do the heavens declare in Psalm 19?
kids.explanation: The heavens declare the glory of God; and the firmament sheweth his handywork (Psalm 19:1).

kids.question: What is the law of the LORD sweeter than according to Psalm 19?
kids.explanation: More to be desired are they than gold, yea, than much fine gold: sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb (Psalm 19:10).

kids.question: What does David ask God to cleanse him from at the end of Psalm 19?
kids.explanation: Cleanse thou me from secret faults (Psalm 19:12).

teens.question: What does day utter to day and night to night according to Psalm 19?
teens.explanation: Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night sheweth knowledge (Psalm 19:2).

teens.question: The sun is compared to what two things in Psalm 19:5?
teens.option_a: A bridegroom and a strong man to run a race
teens.option_b: A fire and a swift bird
teens.explanation: Which is as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber, and rejoiceth as a strong man to run a race (Psalm 19:5).

teens.question: What six terms does David use for God's word in Psalm 19:7-9?
teens.option_a: Law, testimony, statutes, commandment, fear, judgments
teens.option_b: Word, precept, decree, covenant, promise, blessing
teens.option_c: Truth, wisdom, light, life, peace, joy
teens.option_d: Commandment, law, word, saying, oracle, voice
teens.explanation: The law of the LORD, the testimony of the LORD, the statutes of the LORD, the commandment of the LORD, the fear of the LORD, the judgments of the LORD (Psalm 19:7-9).

teens.question: What does David pray would be acceptable in God's sight in Psalm 19:14?
teens.option_a: The words of my mouth, and the meditation of my heart
teens.explanation: Let the words of my mouth, and the meditation of my heart, be acceptable in thy sight, O LORD, my strength, and my redeemer (Psalm 19:14).

teens.question: What does David say "there is no speech nor language" about in Psalm 19:3?
teens.option_b: There is no place where their voice is not heard
teens.explanation: There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard (Psalm 19:3).

adults.question: How does Psalm 19 divide into two major parts, and what theme unites them?
adults.option_a: No divisions exist — it is one continuous poem
adults.option_b: Part 1 (vv.1-6) is general revelation through creation; Part 2 (vv.7-14) is special revelation through the law — both are God's self-disclosure
adults.option_c: Part 1 is lament; Part 2 is praise
adults.option_d: Part 1 is about David; Part 2 is about Israel
adults.explanation: Psalm 19 divides into creation revelation (vv.1-6) and word revelation (vv.7-14), united by the theme of God's self-disclosure in nature and Scripture.

adults.question: What does "there is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard" in Psalm 19:3 teach about general revelation?
adults.option_a: The heavens literally speak in all languages
adults.option_b: Creation's witness transcends all linguistic and cultural barriers — it is universal and without excuse
adults.option_c: The heavens speak only to believers
adults.option_d: Creation's voice requires interpretation by priests
adults.explanation: There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard (Psalm 19:3) — creation's testimony to God is universal, forming the basis of Paul's argument in Romans 1:20.

adults.question: How does Paul allude to Psalm 19:4 in Romans 10:18?
adults.option_b: He applies the worldwide reach of creation's testimony to the worldwide reach of the gospel — "their sound went into all the earth"
adults.option_c: To prove angels preach the gospel
adults.option_d: To prove the law was universal
adults.explanation: Paul quotes "their sound went into all the earth" (Psalm 19:4) in Romans 10:18 to argue that the gospel, like creation's voice, has gone into all the world.

adults.question: What six pairs of quality-and-effect are described for God's word in Psalm 19:7-9?
adults.option_a: Six synonyms for the law with no associated effects
adults.option_b: Perfect (converting the soul), sure (making wise the simple), right (rejoicing the heart), pure (enlightening the eyes), clean (enduring for ever), true and righteous altogether
adults.option_c: Holy, mighty, eternal, good, gracious, powerful
adults.option_d: Faithful, true, just, merciful, righteous, perfect
adults.explanation: Psalm 19:7-9 lists six pairs: law=perfect/converting soul, testimony=sure/wise simple, statutes=right/rejoicing heart, commandment=pure/enlightening eyes, fear=clean/enduring, judgments=true/righteous.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the law being "more to be desired than gold" and "sweeter than honey" in Psalm 19:10?
adults.option_a: Material wealth and sweet food should be avoided
adults.option_b: God's word surpasses the highest material value (gold) and the greatest sensory pleasure (honey) — it is the supreme desirable of life
adults.option_c: The psalmist is describing a literal reward for obedience
adults.option_d: The law was written on gold tablets
adults.explanation: More to be desired are they than gold...sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb (Psalm 19:10) — two superlatives establishing God's word as life's supreme treasure.

adults.question: What does "Who can understand his errors? cleanse thou me from secret faults" in Psalm 19:12 teach about human moral awareness?
adults.option_b: Human beings cannot fully perceive the depth of their own sin — God's law exposes faults hidden even from one's own self-awareness
adults.option_c: Secret sins are unforgivable
adults.option_d: David had committed specific hidden crimes
adults.explanation: Who can understand his errors? cleanse thou me from secret faults (Psalm 19:12) — the law's perfect standard reveals sins the conscience did not register.

adults.question: What are "presumptuous sins" in Psalm 19:13, and why does David specifically pray against them?
adults.option_b: Deliberate, high-handed, wilful rebellion — sins committed with a raised fist against God's known will
adults.option_c: Sins committed in ignorance
adults.option_d: Sins of sexual immorality
adults.explanation: Keep back thy servant also from presumptuous sins; let them not have dominion over me (Psalm 19:13) — presumptuous sins are wilful, deliberate transgressions that lead to moral domination.

adults.question: What does David call God in the final verse of Psalm 19:14, and what is the significance of this closing title?
adults.option_b: My strength and my redeemer — joining the power to sustain with the grace to redeem as the twin grounds of the prayer
adults.option_c: The holy one of Israel
adults.option_d: My rock and my fortress
adults.explanation: "O LORD, my strength, and my redeemer" (Psalm 19:14) — tsuri (my rock/strength) with go'eli (my kinsman-redeemer), combining power and redemptive grace.

kids.question: What does Psalm 20 ask God to do for the king in the day of trouble?
kids.option_a: Hear him in the day of trouble
kids.explanation: The LORD hear thee in the day of trouble; the name of the God of Jacob defend thee (Psalm 20:1).

kids.question: What do some people trust in and what does David trust in?
kids.option_a: Some trust in chariots and horses; we remember the name of the LORD
kids.option_b: Some trust in gold; we trust in prayer
kids.option_c: Some trust in armies; we trust in prophecy
kids.option_d: Some trust in kings; we trust in God's promises
kids.explanation: Some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD our God (Psalm 20:7).

kids.question: What does the psalm ask God to grant the king?
kids.explanation: The LORD fulfil all thy petitions (Psalm 20:5).

teens.question: From where does God send help according to Psalm 20:2?
teens.explanation: Send thee help from the sanctuary, and strengthen thee out of Zion (Psalm 20:2).

teens.question: What does the congregation say they will do in the name of their God in Psalm 20:5?
teens.option_a: We will rejoice in thy salvation and set up our banners
teens.explanation: We will rejoice in thy salvation, and in the name of our God we will set up our banners (Psalm 20:5).

teens.question: What does the LORD remember according to Psalm 20:3?
teens.option_a: All thy offerings, and accept thy burnt sacrifice
teens.explanation: Remember all thy offerings, and accept thy burnt sacrifice (Psalm 20:3).

teens.question: What happens to those who trust in chariots and horses?
teens.option_a: They are brought down and fallen
teens.option_c: They are scattered by God's wind
teens.explanation: They are brought down and fallen: but we are risen, and stand upright (Psalm 20:8).

teens.question: What is the final petition of Psalm 20:9?
teens.option_a: Save, LORD: let the king hear us when we call
teens.option_b: Give victory to your servant the king
teens.option_d: Send your angel before us into battle
teens.explanation: Save, LORD: let the king hear us when we call (Psalm 20:9).

adults.question: What is the liturgical setting of Psalm 20, and how does its structure reflect that setting?
adults.option_b: A royal pre-battle liturgy — a congregation prays for the king (vv.1-5), a priest or prophet declares divine assurance (vv.6-8), and a closing petition is made (v.9)
adults.option_c: A thanksgiving psalm after victory
adults.option_d: A coronation psalm for a new king
adults.explanation: Psalm 20 is a royal pre-battle liturgy: congregational intercession (vv.1-5), a confident divine oracle (vv.6-8), and final petition (v.9).

adults.question: What does "the name of the God of Jacob defend thee" in Psalm 20:1 reveal about the concept of divine name in Israel?
adults.option_b: The divine name represents God's character, covenant, and power — invoking it is appealing to the full weight of God's self-revealed identity and covenant promise
adults.option_c: It is a title only for Jacob's descendants
adults.option_d: It is a liturgical phrase without specific meaning
adults.explanation: The name of the God of Jacob (Psalm 20:1) — in Hebrew thought the divine name encapsulates God's entire character and covenant faithfulness.

adults.question: What does "set up our banners" in Psalm 20:5 signify in the context of ancient Near Eastern warfare?
adults.option_b: Raising military standards in the name of God — claiming victory and territory in God's name rather than human military might
adults.option_c: Making offerings to the LORD
adults.option_d: Proclaiming a national holiday
adults.explanation: In the name of our God we will set up our banners (Psalm 20:5) — military banners (standards) proclaimed ownership and victory in the name of one's deity.

adults.question: How does Psalm 20:6 function as a divine oracle within the congregational prayer?
adults.option_b: It is a sudden shift from petition to confident assurance — "Now know I that the LORD saveth his anointed" — likely spoken by a priest or prophet
adults.option_c: It is David speaking of himself
adults.option_d: It is the king's personal testimony
adults.explanation: Now know I that the LORD saveth his anointed (Psalm 20:6) — a sudden first-person oracle of assurance, likely a priestly or prophetic response to the congregation's prayer.

adults.question: What is the theological principle behind "some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD" in Psalm 20:7?
adults.option_b: That ultimate security rests not in military technology but in the covenant God — trust must be in the divine name, not in human power
adults.option_c: That Israel never used military equipment
adults.option_d: That chariots and horses are forbidden by the law
adults.explanation: Some trust in chariots and some in horses (Psalm 20:7) — the contrast is not between preparation and laziness, but between ultimate trust in military power versus trust in the covenant God.

adults.question: How does the law of the king in Deuteronomy 17:16 relate to Psalm 20:7?
adults.option_b: Deuteronomy 17:16 forbids the king from multiplying horses — Psalm 20:7 reflects this principle, grounding military security in God rather than cavalry
adults.option_c: Deuteronomy contradicts Psalm 20
adults.option_d: The law permits horses for the king only
adults.explanation: Deuteronomy 17:16 warned the king not to multiply horses — Psalm 20:7's contrast between trusting in horses versus the LORD's name applies this principle liturgically.

adults.question: What does "his holy heaven" in Psalm 20:6 add to the theology of divine transcendence and immanence in the psalm?
adults.option_a: God is only in heaven and not near
adults.option_b: God's help comes from the sanctuary (immanent, v.2) and from holy heaven (transcendent, v.6) — both his nearness and his exalted majesty are the source of help
adults.option_c: The sanctuary is in heaven only
adults.option_d: God's help comes exclusively through the temple
adults.explanation: Help from the sanctuary (v.2) and from his holy heaven (v.6) — Psalm 20 affirms both God's immanent presence in the earthly sanctuary and his transcendent rule from heaven.

adults.question: How does Psalm 20 anticipate the theology of the New Testament regarding prayer for leaders?
adults.option_a: It has no New Testament connection
adults.option_b: It models intercession for rulers and leaders, anticipating Paul's instruction to pray for kings and all in authority (1 Timothy 2:1-2)
adults.option_c: It teaches that kings need no prayer
adults.option_d: It is only relevant to Israel's monarchy
adults.explanation: Psalm 20's congregational intercession for the king anticipates Paul's instruction: "supplications, prayers...be made for all men; for kings, and for all that are in authority" (1 Timothy 2:1-2).

kids.question: What does the king rejoice in according to Psalm 21:1?
kids.explanation: The king shall joy in thy strength, O LORD; and in thy salvation how greatly shall he rejoice! (Psalm 21:1).

kids.question: What did God set on the king's head in Psalm 21:3?
kids.explanation: Thou settest a crown of pure gold on his head (Psalm 21:3).

kids.question: What did the king ask God for and what did God give him?
kids.option_a: He asked for life, and God gave it to him
kids.option_b: He asked for wealth, and God gave it to him
kids.option_c: He asked for victory, and God gave it to him
kids.option_d: He asked for wisdom, and God gave it to him
kids.explanation: He asked life of thee, and thou gavest it him, even length of days for ever and ever (Psalm 21:4).

teens.question: What blessing did God not withhold from the king in Psalm 21:2?
teens.option_a: Thou hast not withholden the request of his lips
teens.explanation: Thou hast given him his heart's desire, and hast not withholden the request of his lips (Psalm 21:2).

teens.question: What is the king's glory described as in Psalm 21:5?
teens.option_a: His glory is great in thy salvation
teens.option_d: His glory shines like the sun
teens.explanation: His glory is great in thy salvation: honour and majesty hast thou laid upon him (Psalm 21:5).

teens.question: What does God make the king in Psalm 21:6?
teens.explanation: For thou hast made him most blessed for ever: thou hast made him exceeding glad with thy countenance (Psalm 21:6).

teens.question: What will happen to the enemies of God's king according to Psalm 21:9?
teens.option_a: Thou shalt make them as a fiery oven in the time of thine anger
teens.explanation: Thou shalt make them as a fiery oven in the time of thine anger (Psalm 21:9).

teens.question: Why does the king trust in the LORD and not be moved according to Psalm 21:7?
teens.option_a: Because the king trusteth in the LORD and through the mercy of the most High he shall not be moved
teens.option_b: Because the king has a great army
teens.option_c: Because the king has strong walls around his city
teens.option_d: Because the king is righteous above all others
teens.explanation: For the king trusteth in the LORD, and through the mercy of the most High he shall not be moved (Psalm 21:7).

adults.question: How does Psalm 21 relate to Psalm 20 structurally?
adults.option_b: Psalm 20 is the pre-battle prayer; Psalm 21 is the post-battle thanksgiving — the two form a royal diptych of intercession and praise
adults.option_c: Psalm 21 corrects Psalm 20
adults.option_d: Psalm 20 celebrates victory; Psalm 21 anticipates battle
adults.explanation: Psalm 20 is the congregational prayer before battle; Psalm 21 is the thanksgiving after — together they form a liturgical diptych around the king's warfare.

adults.question: What does "the mercy of the most High" in Psalm 21:7 emphasize as the basis of the king's unshakeable stability?
adults.option_b: God's hesed (covenant lovingkindness) is the ultimate reason the king cannot be moved — not human virtue
adults.option_c: The king's righteousness
adults.option_d: The king's wisdom in governing
adults.explanation: Through the mercy of the most High he shall not be moved (Psalm 21:7) — the king's stability is rooted in God's covenant faithfulness, not the king's own merit.

adults.question: What does the crown of pure gold in Psalm 21:3 symbolize in the Davidic covenant context?
adults.option_b: Divine legitimization and blessing — God himself crowns the king, confirming the Davidic covenant
adults.option_c: Military conquest
adults.option_d: The king's personal righteousness
adults.explanation: Thou settest a crown of pure gold on his head (Psalm 21:3) — the golden crown signifies God's direct authorization of the Davidic king under the covenant.

adults.question: What does "length of days for ever and ever" in Psalm 21:4 ultimately point to beyond the historical king?
adults.option_b: The eternal life of the Messianic king — fulfilled in Christ's resurrection and eternal reign
adults.option_c: A dynasty of many kings
adults.option_d: Immortality achieved through obedience
adults.explanation: He asked life of thee, and thou gavest it him, even length of days for ever and ever (Psalm 21:4) — pointing to the Messianic king's eternal life through resurrection.

adults.question: What does "made him exceeding glad with thy countenance" in Psalm 21:6 reveal about the nature of divine blessing?
adults.option_b: The highest joy is not material but relational — the face (countenance) of God is itself the supreme gift
adults.option_c: Victory in battle is the greatest blessing
adults.option_d: Wisdom and knowledge are the greatest gifts
adults.explanation: Thou hast made him exceeding glad with thy countenance (Psalm 21:6) — the divine face shining on the king is identified as the source of supreme joy.

adults.question: What does the fiery oven imagery in Psalm 21:9 recall from the Pentateuch?
adults.option_b: The smoke of a furnace from Sodom's destruction (Genesis 19:28) — divine judgment consuming the enemies
adults.option_c: The pillar of fire in the wilderness
adults.option_d: The fire on Mount Sinai
adults.explanation: Thou shalt make them as a fiery oven in the time of thine anger (Psalm 21:9) — recalling the smoke of a furnace over Sodom (Genesis 19:28) as divine judgment imagery.

adults.question: How does the destruction of the king's enemies's offspring in Psalm 21:10 function in ancient Near Eastern royal theology?
adults.option_b: Complete destruction of the enemy lineage ensures no future threat — a standard expression of total divine victory in royal psalms
adults.option_c: It applies only to specific nations
adults.option_d: It is a metaphor for spiritual opposition
adults.explanation: Their fruit shalt thou destroy from the earth (Psalm 21:10) — complete destruction of enemy offspring reflects ancient Near Eastern total victory language in royal contexts.

adults.question: How does the congregation's praise in Psalm 21:13 ("Be thou exalted, LORD") appropriately conclude a royal psalm?
adults.option_a: It redirects all glory from the king to God
adults.option_b: It praises the king's military skill
adults.option_c: It prays for the king's next battle
adults.option_d: It closes with a lament
adults.explanation: Be thou exalted, LORD, in thine own strength: so will we sing and praise thy power (Psalm 21:13) — the royal psalm ends by redirecting all honour from the king to God himself.

kids.question: What cry does the suffering person make at the beginning of Psalm 22?
kids.option_a: My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?
kids.option_b: Save me, O God, for the waters are deep
kids.option_c: How long wilt thou forget me, O LORD?
kids.option_d: Out of the depths I cry to thee
kids.explanation: My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me? (Psalm 22:1).

kids.question: Who spoke the opening words of Psalm 22 on the cross?
kids.explanation: Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying...My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me? (Matthew 27:46, quoting Psalm 22:1).

kids.question: What did the enemies do with the suffering person's garments in Psalm 22?
kids.option_a: They cast lots upon my vesture
kids.explanation: They part my garments among them, and cast lots upon my vesture (Psalm 22:18).

teens.question: What do the enemies say as they shake their heads and mock the sufferer in Psalm 22?
teens.option_a: He trusted on the LORD that he would deliver him
teens.option_b: There is no help for him in God
teens.option_c: His God has abandoned him forever
teens.option_d: Let him call on his God if he can
teens.explanation: He trusted on the LORD that he would deliver him: let him deliver him, seeing he delighted in him (Psalm 22:8).

teens.question: What does the sufferer say encircles him in Psalm 22:12-13?
teens.option_a: Many bulls and a ravening and a roaring lion
teens.option_d: Walls of fire and walls of ice
teens.explanation: Many bulls have compassed me...they gaped upon me with their mouths, as a ravening and a roaring lion (Psalm 22:12-13).

teens.question: What happens to the body of the sufferer in Psalm 22:14?
teens.option_a: I am poured out like water and all my bones are out of joint
teens.option_b: My hands and feet are bound
teens.option_c: My eyes are covered with darkness
teens.explanation: I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint: my heart is like wax; it is melted in the midst of my bowels (Psalm 22:14).

teens.question: What does the sufferer say about his hands and feet in Psalm 22:16?
teens.option_a: They have pierced my hands and my feet
teens.option_d: They are too weak to move
teens.explanation: They pierced my hands and my feet (Psalm 22:16).

teens.question: What does Psalm 22 say shall be declared to future generations?
teens.option_a: That he hath done this — his righteousness
teens.option_b: That God is merciful to the poor
teens.option_c: That the LORD saves his anointed
teens.explanation: They shall come, and shall declare his righteousness unto a people that shall be born, that he hath done this (Psalm 22:31).

adults.question: How does Psalm 22 function as a Messianic psalm, given that it was written by David yet fulfilled in Christ?
adults.option_a: David was describing his own experience only
adults.option_b: David's intense personal suffering became a prophetic template fulfilled in Christ's crucifixion — the details (pierced hands/feet, divided garments, lots, scorning) find precise fulfilment in the Gospel accounts
adults.option_c: The psalm has no Messianic meaning
adults.option_d: The Gospels are allegorically interpreting David's words
adults.explanation: Psalm 22 is a typological/prophetic psalm — David's suffering is real, but the Spirit carried his words beyond his experience to prefigure Christ's crucifixion with stunning precision.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Jesus quoting Psalm 22:1 from the cross in Matthew 27:46?
adults.option_a: Jesus was expressing despair and lack of faith
adults.option_b: Jesus was identifying himself as the Psalm 22 sufferer, announcing to those who knew the Psalms that the crucifixion was the fulfilment of this scripture
adults.option_c: Jesus was quoting scripture from memory without specific intent
adults.option_d: Jesus was confused about his identity
adults.explanation: By quoting Psalm 22:1, Jesus was identifying himself as the prophesied sufferer of Psalm 22, announcing its fulfilment to those who knew the text (Matthew 27:46).

adults.question: What does "I am a worm, and no man" in Psalm 22:6 convey about the sufferer's condition?
adults.option_a: The sufferer has become physically diseased
adults.option_b: The most extreme humiliation — a worm had no legal standing, could be crushed underfoot, representing utter degradation and contempt
adults.option_c: The sufferer is confessing sin
adults.option_d: The sufferer is exaggerating his poverty
adults.explanation: I am a worm, and no man; a reproach of men, and despised of the people (Psalm 22:6) — complete human degradation, applied by Paul to Christ's humiliation (Philippians 2:7-8).

adults.question: How does John 19:24 connect the dividing of garments and casting of lots to Psalm 22:18?
adults.option_b: John explicitly states this happened "that the scripture might be fulfilled" quoting Psalm 22:18 — identifying it as direct prophetic fulfilment
adults.option_c: John is making a loose allusion only
adults.option_d: The garments detail is only symbolic
adults.explanation: John 19:24 — "That the scripture might be fulfilled, which saith, They parted my raiment among them, and for my vesture they did cast lots" — directly fulfils Psalm 22:18.

adults.question: What is the structural turning point in Psalm 22, and what causes the shift?
adults.option_b: At verse 24 — "For he hath not despised nor abhorred the affliction of the afflicted" — the mood shifts from abandonment to praise as God's faithfulness is affirmed
adults.option_c: The shift happens at verse 1
adults.option_d: The psalm has no shift — it is entirely lament
adults.explanation: The turning point is verse 24 — "he hath not despised nor abhorred the affliction of the afflicted" — from which point the psalm moves into global praise (vv.25-31).

adults.question: What does "they shall come and shall declare his righteousness unto a people that shall be born" in Psalm 22:31 anticipate?
adults.option_a: The birth of Israel's next king
adults.option_b: The worldwide proclamation of the gospel — the suffering-and-vindication story of the Messianic servant declared to all future generations
adults.option_c: The writing of the Psalms
adults.option_d: The rebuilding of Jerusalem after exile
adults.explanation: Psalm 22:31 looks forward to future generations declaring "he hath done this" — anticipating the worldwide gospel proclamation of Christ's death and resurrection.

adults.question: What does "they shall come and shall declare his righteousness" mean by "his righteousness" in Psalm 22:31?
adults.option_b: God's saving righteousness — his righteous action in vindicating the sufferer through resurrection, the basis of gospel proclamation
adults.option_c: Israel's national righteousness
adults.option_d: The sufferer's personal righteousness
adults.explanation: "His righteousness" (Psalm 22:31) — God's righteousness refers to his saving, vindicating action — his righteous intervention on behalf of the sufferer.

adults.question: What does the scope of praise in Psalm 22:27-29 ("all the ends of the world...all the kindreds of the nations") reveal about the Messianic suffering?
adults.option_a: It is limited to Israel only
adults.option_b: The suffering of the Messianic figure produces universal, worldwide worship — Gentile nations included — previewing the global scope of the gospel
adults.option_c: It is a hyperbolic expression without specific meaning
adults.option_d: It refers to David's empire
adults.explanation: All the ends of the world shall remember and turn unto the LORD (Psalm 22:27) — the Messianic suffering produces worldwide worship, fulfilled in the global church.

kids.question: How does Psalm 23 begin?
kids.explanation: The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want (Psalm 23:1).

kids.question: Where does the shepherd lead the sheep to rest?
kids.explanation: He maketh me to lie down in green pastures: he leadeth me beside the still waters (Psalm 23:2).

kids.question: What overflows in Psalm 23?
kids.explanation: Thou preparest a table before me in the presence of mine enemies: thou anointest my head with oil; my cup runneth over (Psalm 23:5).

teens.question: What valley does the psalmist walk through without fear?
teens.option_a: The valley of the shadow of death
teens.explanation: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil: for thou art with me (Psalm 23:4).

teens.question: What two items does the shepherd carry in Psalm 23:4?
teens.explanation: Thy rod and thy staff they comfort me (Psalm 23:4).

teens.question: Where does God prepare a table for the psalmist?
teens.option_a: In the presence of mine enemies
teens.option_b: In the house of the LORD
teens.explanation: Thou preparest a table before me in the presence of mine enemies (Psalm 23:5).

teens.question: What does the shepherd do to the sheep's soul in Psalm 23:3?
teens.explanation: He restoreth my soul: he leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake (Psalm 23:3).

teens.question: What will follow the psalmist all the days of his life?
teens.explanation: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life (Psalm 23:6).

adults.question: What is the dual metaphor of Psalm 23, and at what point does the metaphor shift?
adults.option_a: It is entirely a shepherd metaphor
adults.option_b: Verses 1-4 use the shepherd/sheep metaphor; verses 5-6 shift to the host/guest metaphor — God as a generous host preparing a banquet
adults.option_c: It is entirely a banquet metaphor
adults.option_d: Verses 1-3 describe creation; verses 4-6 describe judgment
adults.explanation: Psalm 23 uses two metaphors: the shepherd and his sheep (vv.1-4) and the gracious host and honoured guest (vv.5-6), both expressing God's comprehensive provision and care.

adults.question: What is the significance of "for his name's sake" in Psalm 23:3?
adults.option_a: David is claiming the LORD's name as a charm
adults.option_b: God's guidance of the righteous paths is grounded in his own covenant character and reputation — not David's merit
adults.option_c: God acts for his own glory in a self-serving way
adults.option_d: The name is a title of power only
adults.explanation: He leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake (Psalm 23:3) — God's guidance is motivated by his own character and covenant faithfulness.

adults.question: What does "the valley of the shadow of death" (tsalmaveth) convey in Hebrew?
adults.option_b: Deep darkness or the shadow of death — expressing the most dangerous and terrifying situations of life
adults.option_c: A valley used for burial
adults.option_d: A metaphor for spiritual darkness only
adults.explanation: Tsalmaveth (Psalm 23:4) combines darkness and death — the deepest, most life-threatening darkness, not merely hardship but mortal danger.

adults.question: What is the significance of anointing the head with oil in Psalm 23:5 in the ancient Near Eastern context?
adults.option_b: It was the mark of an honoured guest — oil was poured on a guest's head as a sign of special welcome and dignity
adults.option_c: It was a priestly rite
adults.option_d: It symbolizes healing from sickness
adults.explanation: Thou anointest my head with oil (Psalm 23:5) — in ancient Near Eastern hospitality, anointing a guest's head with oil was a mark of highest honour and welcome.

adults.question: What does "goodness and mercy shall follow me" convey differently from saying "goodness and mercy shall be with me"?
adults.option_a: No difference — they mean the same
adults.option_b: To "follow" (radaph — to pursue) suggests goodness and mercy actively chase the psalmist — they are not passive companions but aggressive pursuers
adults.option_c: Following implies they come after problems
adults.option_d: Following is a weaker form of accompaniment
adults.explanation: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me (Psalm 23:6) — the Hebrew radaph means to pursue or chase, suggesting God's goodness actively pursues the psalmist.

adults.question: How does Jesus' declaration "I am the good shepherd" in John 10:11 relate to Psalm 23?
adults.option_b: Jesus directly applies the divine shepherd role of Psalm 23 to himself, claiming to be the fulfillment of YHWH the shepherd
adults.option_c: Jesus was quoting Ezekiel 34 only
adults.option_d: Jesus was describing an ideal human leader
adults.explanation: "I am the good shepherd" (John 10:11) — Jesus identifies himself as the YHWH-shepherd of Psalm 23, claiming divine identity and fulfilling the Messianic shepherd prophecies.

adults.question: What does preparing a table "in the presence of mine enemies" in Psalm 23:5 demonstrate about God's protection?
adults.option_a: The enemies are irrelevant to God
adults.option_b: God's provision is so complete and his protection so total that the psalmist can feast with enemies nearby — a declaration of unshakeable security
adults.option_c: The enemies are invited to share the meal
adults.option_d: The table is in a safe place hidden from enemies
adults.explanation: God prepares a banquet in plain view of enemies (Psalm 23:5) — demonstrating that God's protection is absolute and the enemies' presence cannot diminish or threaten his provision.

adults.question: What does "I will dwell in the house of the LORD for ever" in Psalm 23:6 express as the psalm's ultimate aspiration?
adults.option_a: Permanent temple residence as a priest
adults.option_b: Eternal fellowship with God — the ultimate destination of the shepherd's leading and the host's welcoming
adults.option_c: Continued earthly life in Jerusalem
adults.option_d: A promise of reincarnation
adults.explanation: I will dwell in the house of the LORD for ever (Psalm 23:6) — the psalm's goal is not mere safety but eternal communion with God, the greatest good of all.

kids.question: Who does Psalm 24 say the earth belongs to?
kids.explanation: The earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof; the world, and they that dwell therein (Psalm 24:1).

kids.question: What are the gates told to do in Psalm 24?
kids.explanation: Lift up your heads, O ye gates; and be ye lift up, ye everlasting doors; and the King of glory shall come in (Psalm 24:7).

kids.question: What is the King called in Psalm 24?
kids.explanation: Who is this King of glory? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle (Psalm 24:8).

teens.question: How did God found the earth according to Psalm 24:2?
teens.explanation: For he hath founded it upon the seas, and established it upon the floods (Psalm 24:2).

teens.question: Who shall ascend into the hill of the LORD according to Psalm 24:4?
teens.option_a: He that hath clean hands and a pure heart
teens.option_b: He that hath offered the greatest sacrifice
teens.option_c: He that hath served in the temple
teens.option_d: He that hath kept all the feasts
teens.explanation: He that hath clean hands, and a pure heart; who hath not lifted up his soul unto vanity, nor sworn deceitfully (Psalm 24:4).

teens.question: What will the person with clean hands and a pure heart receive?
teens.option_a: Blessing from the LORD and righteousness from the God of his salvation
teens.explanation: He shall receive the blessing from the LORD, and righteousness from the God of his salvation (Psalm 24:5).

teens.question: Who is the King of glory described as in Psalm 24:8?
teens.option_a: The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle
teens.option_b: The LORD of all the earth
teens.option_c: The LORD who sits above the cherubim
teens.option_d: The LORD who made the heavens
teens.explanation: Who is this King of glory? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle (Psalm 24:8).

teens.question: What is said about those who seek God's face in Psalm 24:6?
teens.option_a: This is the generation of them that seek him
teens.option_b: They shall be blessed above all
teens.option_c: They shall dwell on God's holy hill
teens.option_d: They shall receive a great reward
teens.explanation: This is the generation of them that seek him, that seek thy face, O Jacob (Psalm 24:6).

adults.question: What is the proposed liturgical context of Psalm 24 in Jewish tradition?
adults.option_b: A processional psalm for the ark being brought to Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6) or a Sabbath morning liturgy
adults.option_c: A psalm for the Day of Atonement
adults.option_d: A psalm for the Feast of Tabernacles
adults.explanation: Psalm 24 is traditionally associated with the bringing of the ark to Jerusalem (2 Samuel 6) and is the Sabbath morning psalm in Jewish liturgy.

adults.question: What is the cosmological claim of "he hath founded it upon the seas" in Psalm 24:2, and how does it subvert ancient Near Eastern mythology?
adults.option_a: Israel believed the sea was the source of all life
adults.option_b: In ancient Near Eastern myths, the sea was a primordial force opposed to the creator gods — Psalm 24 asserts that God simply founded the earth on the seas, completely ruling them
adults.option_c: It describes how God created the oceans
adults.option_d: It proves the earth is flat
adults.explanation: He hath founded it upon the seas (Psalm 24:2) — while ancient Near Eastern gods fought the sea, Israel's God simply uses the seas as a foundation, asserting absolute sovereignty.

adults.question: What does "clean hands and a pure heart" in Psalm 24:4 represent as a complete description of moral integrity?
adults.option_b: Outward action (clean hands) and inner motive (pure heart) — the full integration of external conduct and internal character
adults.option_c: Only ritual washings
adults.option_d: Only inner spiritual states
adults.explanation: Clean hands (external action) and pure heart (internal motive) in Psalm 24:4 — the complete picture of integrity: doing right for right reasons.

adults.question: How is the question-and-answer liturgy "Who is this King of glory?" in Psalm 24:8-10 thought to have functioned?
adults.option_b: A processional liturgy where the question was called from inside the gates and answered by the approaching procession bearing the ark
adults.option_c: A song for the temple choir only
adults.option_d: A private meditation of David
adults.explanation: The antiphonal call-and-response in Psalm 24:7-10 was likely a processional liturgy — voices inside the gates asking, the procession outside answering — as the ark entered the city.

adults.question: What does the repeated "lift up your heads, O ye gates" (Psalm 24:7, 9) accomplish liturgically and theologically?
adults.option_b: The repetition creates an intensifying dramatic effect — the King of glory demands and deserves the fullest possible welcome from all creation
adults.option_c: It was sung twice because the first attempt was insufficient
adults.option_d: It is a poetic device without theological meaning
adults.explanation: The repetition of "lift up your heads" (Psalm 24:7, 9) creates a crescendo, intensifying the drama of the divine King's entry and demanding the universe's fullest welcome.

adults.question: How is Psalm 24 applied in Christian worship, particularly in relation to Ascension and Advent?
adults.option_b: It is applied to Christ's ascension — the King of glory entering heaven's gates — and to his second coming; early church read it in this Messianic framework
adults.option_c: It applies only to the temple liturgy
adults.option_d: It applies only to the Davidic monarchy
adults.explanation: Psalm 24 is applied in Christian tradition to Christ's ascension (the King of glory ascending to heaven) and his second coming, citing patristic and early Christian interpretation.

adults.question: What does "the LORD of hosts" as the title for the King of glory in Psalm 24:10 convey?
adults.option_b: God commands the heavenly armies — the divine warrior title expressing his supreme authority over all spiritual and physical powers
adults.option_c: God created many angels
adults.option_d: God rules the nations through earthly kings
adults.explanation: The LORD of hosts, he is the King of glory (Psalm 24:10) — LORD of hosts (YHWH Sabaoth) is the divine warrior title, expressing God's supreme command over all heavenly powers.

adults.question: How does "the fulness thereof" in Psalm 24:1 set the theological foundation for all that follows about who may ascend God's hill?
adults.option_a: It is a poetic filler phrase
adults.option_b: If everything belongs to God — the earth, its contents, and all inhabitants — then God has the absolute right to define the conditions of approach and worship
adults.option_c: It means the earth is too full of people
adults.option_d: It is an agricultural reference to harvest
adults.explanation: The earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof (Psalm 24:1) — because God owns everything, he has absolute authority to set the moral conditions for approaching him.

kids.question: What does David ask God to show him in Psalm 25?
kids.option_a: Shew me thy ways, O LORD
kids.explanation: Shew me thy ways, O LORD; teach me thy paths (Psalm 25:4).

kids.question: What does David ask God NOT to remember?
kids.explanation: Remember not the sins of my youth, nor my transgressions: according to thy mercy remember thou me (Psalm 25:7).

kids.question: What does David lift up to God in Psalm 25?
kids.explanation: Unto thee, O LORD, do I lift up my soul (Psalm 25:1).

teens.question: What does David say about waiting on the LORD in Psalm 25?
teens.option_a: On thee do I wait all the day
teens.option_b: I will wait until the morning
teens.option_c: I wait with great patience for thee
teens.option_d: I wait in the temple for thy answer
teens.explanation: Lead me in thy truth, and teach me: for thou art the God of my salvation; on thee do I wait all the day (Psalm 25:5).

teens.question: Who does God guide in judgment and teach his way according to Psalm 25:9?
teens.explanation: The meek will he guide in judgment: and the meek will he teach his way (Psalm 25:9).

teens.question: What is the secret of the LORD and who is it with in Psalm 25:14?
teens.option_a: The secret of the LORD is with them that fear him
teens.option_b: The secret of the LORD is hidden in the law
teens.option_c: The secret of the LORD is in the temple
teens.option_d: The secret of the LORD is revealed to prophets
teens.explanation: The secret of the LORD is with them that fear him; and he will shew them his covenant (Psalm 25:14).

teens.question: What does David ask God to look upon in Psalm 25:18?
teens.option_a: Mine affliction and my pain; and forgive all my sins
teens.explanation: Look upon mine affliction and my pain; and forgive all my sins (Psalm 25:18).

teens.question: What does David say his eyes are ever toward in Psalm 25?
teens.explanation: Mine eyes are ever toward the LORD; for he shall pluck my feet out of the net (Psalm 25:15).

adults.question: What literary structure does Psalm 25 employ in the original Hebrew?
adults.option_b: An alphabetic acrostic — each verse begins with successive letters of the Hebrew alphabet
adults.option_c: A triplet refrain structure
adults.option_d: A question-and-answer dialogue
adults.explanation: Psalm 25 is an alphabetic acrostic in Hebrew — each verse beginning with successive letters from Aleph to Taw, demonstrating both artistry and completeness.

adults.question: What does the "secret of the LORD" (sod YHWH) in Psalm 25:14 refer to?
adults.option_a: Hidden mystical knowledge reserved for prophets
adults.option_b: Intimate covenantal friendship — the counsel and communion God shares with those who fear him
adults.option_c: Secret codes in the law
adults.option_d: Hidden knowledge of the future
adults.explanation: The secret of the LORD is with them that fear him (Psalm 25:14) — sod (secret/council) refers to intimate friendship and covenantal communion, as in Job 29:4 and Proverbs 3:32.

adults.question: What does David's prayer "remember not the sins of my youth" in Psalm 25:7 reveal about the relationship between God's memory and forgiveness?
adults.option_b: God's "not remembering" is a covenantal act — choosing not to hold sins against the pardoned person, based on his mercy
adults.option_c: Youth sins are less serious
adults.option_d: God only forgives sins of adulthood
adults.explanation: Remember not the sins of my youth (Psalm 25:7) — God's "not remembering" is a covenantal act of forgiveness, not literal amnesia, grounded in his mercy.

adults.question: What does David's repeated emphasis on "teach me," "lead me," and "guide me" in Psalm 25 reveal about the nature of the life of faith?
adults.option_b: Living faithfully requires continuous divine instruction and guidance — it is not self-directed but God-directed
adults.option_c: It shows David was too dependent on others
adults.option_d: It shows that God's ways must be learned only once
adults.explanation: The repeated petitions to be taught and guided (Psalm 25:4-5, 8-9) show that the life of faith is not self-sufficient but requires constant dependence on God's instruction.

adults.question: What is the relationship between fearing God and receiving his covenant instruction in Psalm 25:12-14?
adults.option_b: Those who fear God receive his guidance, inherit the land, and are shown his covenant — fear of God is the gateway to divine intimacy
adults.option_c: Only priests who fear God receive instruction
adults.option_d: Fearing God brings material reward only
adults.explanation: What man is he that feareth the LORD? him shall he teach...his soul shall dwell at ease...the secret of the LORD is with them that fear him (Psalm 25:12-14).

adults.question: What does "the paths of the LORD are mercy and truth" in Psalm 25:10 assert about God's way of dealing with mankind?
adults.option_a: God's paths are difficult and hard to find
adults.option_b: Every path God leads his people along is characterized by hesed (covenant love) and emet (faithfulness) — the twin pillars of his character
adults.option_c: God demands truth from people before he shows mercy
adults.option_d: God's mercy is for some paths and his truth for others
adults.explanation: All the paths of the LORD are mercy and truth (Psalm 25:10) — hesed and emet (steadfast love and faithfulness) characterize every divine dealing with his people.

adults.question: What does David's closing prayer "Redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles" in Psalm 25:22 do to the personal psalm?
adults.option_b: It broadens the personal lament into a corporate intercession, connecting individual suffering to the national covenant community
adults.option_c: It shows David was thinking of politics only
adults.option_d: It shows the psalm was written by two different authors
adults.explanation: "Redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles" (Psalm 25:22) — the personal psalm ends in corporate intercession, connecting the individual's suffering to the whole covenant people.

adults.question: How does the instruction theme of Psalm 25 connect to the wisdom literature tradition of Israel?
adults.option_a: Psalm 25 has no connection to wisdom literature
adults.option_b: Psalm 25 shares the wisdom tradition's emphasis on fearing God as the beginning of wisdom and seeking instruction in the way of life — connecting prayer and sapiential instruction
adults.option_c: Psalm 25 contradicts wisdom literature
adults.option_d: Only Proverbs contains wisdom themes
adults.explanation: Psalm 25 connects to wisdom literature: fear of God (v.14), divine instruction in "the way" (vv.4-5, 8-9), and the meek receiving guidance — all central wisdom themes.

kids.question: What does David ask God to do to him in Psalm 26?
kids.explanation: Judge me, O LORD; for I have walked in mine integrity (Psalm 26:1).

kids.question: What does David love in Psalm 26?
kids.option_a: The habitation of thy house and the place where thine honour dwelleth
kids.option_b: The mountains and rivers God made
kids.option_c: The songs of the temple singers
kids.explanation: LORD, I have loved the habitation of thy house, and the place where thine honour dwelleth (Psalm 26:8).

kids.question: What does David do around the altar in Psalm 26?
kids.option_b: I will sing praises to thee
kids.option_c: I will offer sacrifices to thee
kids.option_d: I will bring my gifts to thee
kids.explanation: That I may publish with the voice of thanksgiving, and tell of all thy wondrous works. LORD, I have loved the habitation of thy house...I will compass thine altar, O LORD (Psalm 26:7-8, 6).

teens.question: What does David ask God to examine in Psalm 26:2?
teens.option_a: Examine me, O LORD, and prove me; try my reins and my heart
teens.option_b: Examine my words and my deeds
teens.option_c: Examine my enemies and judge them
teens.option_d: Examine my offerings and accept them
teens.explanation: Examine me, O LORD, and prove me; try my reins and my heart (Psalm 26:2).

teens.question: With what kind of men does David say he has NOT sat in Psalm 26:4?
teens.explanation: I have not sat with vain persons, neither will I go in with dissemblers (Psalm 26:4).

teens.question: What congregation does David hate in Psalm 26:5?
teens.explanation: I have hated the congregation of evil doers; and will not sit with the wicked (Psalm 26:5).

teens.question: What does David wash in Psalm 26:6?
teens.explanation: I will wash mine hands in innocency: so will I compass thine altar, O LORD (Psalm 26:6).

teens.question: What does David ask God not to do to him in Psalm 26:9?
teens.option_a: Gather not my soul with sinners
teens.option_b: Take not my life with the wicked
teens.option_c: Send not my soul into darkness
teens.option_d: Cast not me away from thy presence
teens.explanation: Gather not my soul with sinners, nor my life with bloody men (Psalm 26:9).

adults.question: What type of psalm is Psalm 26, and what is unusual about its opening verse?
adults.option_a: A lament with a prayer for help
adults.option_b: A psalm of personal integrity — unusually opening with an appeal to God to judge the psalmist rather than to forgive, based on claimed innocence
adults.option_c: A thanksgiving psalm
adults.option_d: A royal psalm
adults.explanation: Judge me, O LORD; for I have walked in mine integrity (Psalm 26:1) — unusual in that David invites divine judgment based on integrity, not seeking pardon but vindication.

adults.question: What does the judicial language of "judge me" in Psalm 26:1 suggest about the background of this psalm?
adults.option_b: David is under false accusation and is appealing to God as the supreme court to vindicate his integrity against unjust charges
adults.option_c: David is asking God to punish him appropriately
adults.option_d: David is making a philosophical inquiry
adults.explanation: Judge me, O LORD (Psalm 26:1) — the judicial language suggests a specific false accusation context; David is appealing to the divine court for vindication.

adults.question: What does "try my reins and my heart" in Psalm 26:2 mean, and what is the relationship between these two?
adults.option_a: A medical examination of kidneys and heart
adults.option_b: The reins (kidneys) represent the seat of deep motives and emotions; the heart represents the will and intellect — God is asked to examine the total inner person
adults.option_c: The reins represent actions and the heart represents speech
adults.option_d: They are synonyms with no distinction
adults.explanation: Examine me...try my reins and my heart (Psalm 26:2) — the reins (deepest emotions/motives) and heart (will/intellect) together represent the complete inner person laid open before God.

adults.question: What does David's washing of hands in innocency in Psalm 26:6 draw on culturally and theologically?
adults.option_b: The ancient ritual of washing hands to symbolize innocence of a specific charge — as in the declaration of innocence in Deuteronomy 21:6-7
adults.option_c: A temple purification requirement
adults.option_d: A metaphor for repentance only
adults.explanation: I will wash mine hands in innocency (Psalm 26:6) — echoes the judicial hand-washing of innocence in Deuteronomy 21:6-7, declaring freedom from a specific charge.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of David's "I have loved the habitation of thy house" in Psalm 26:8?
adults.option_b: Love for God's dwelling place expresses the heart's deepest longing for God's presence — the sanctuary is where God's glory and honour dwell
adults.option_c: David was a temple architect
adults.option_d: David preferred the temple to the palace
adults.explanation: LORD, I have loved the habitation of thy house, and the place where thine honour dwelleth (Psalm 26:8) — love for God's house is love for God's presence.

adults.question: How does the closing promise "I will walk in mine integrity" in Psalm 26:11 relate to the opening claim of the same in Psalm 26:1?
adults.option_b: The opening claim (v.1) is historical fact; the closing promise (v.11) is renewed commitment — together showing integrity as both past reality and future intention
adults.option_c: The closing is a correction of the opening
adults.option_d: Both statements are aspirational only
adults.explanation: Opening: "I have walked in mine integrity" (Psalm 26:1); closing: "I will walk in mine integrity" (Psalm 26:11) — past integrity and future commitment form an inclusio around the psalm.

adults.question: What does "tell of all thy wondrous works" in Psalm 26:7 reveal about the purpose of approaching God's altar?
adults.option_a: The altar is for silent prayer
adults.option_b: The approach to God's presence is for proclamation — the experience of God's grace produces testimony and the public declaration of his mighty acts
adults.option_c: The altar is only for sacrifice
adults.option_d: Proclamation replaces sacrifice in the new covenant
adults.explanation: That I may publish with the voice of thanksgiving, and tell of all thy wondrous works (Psalm 26:7) — approaching the altar leads to proclamation of God's marvelous deeds.

adults.question: How does Psalm 26's model of integrity-based prayer challenge a purely grace-based understanding of prayer?
adults.option_a: It shows that merit earns God's favour
adults.option_b: It demonstrates that integrity and grace coexist — David appeals to grace ("redeem me and be merciful" v.11) even while claiming integrity; the two are not contradictory
adults.option_c: It proves the law saves
adults.option_d: It contradicts the New Testament's emphasis on grace
adults.explanation: Psalm 26 holds together integrity (vv.1-3, 11) and grace (v.11 "be merciful unto me") — showing that honest integrity-claims and trust in God's mercy are not contradictory in biblical faith.

kids.question: How does Psalm 27 begin?
kids.option_a: The LORD is my light and my salvation
kids.option_c: The LORD is my rock and my fortress
kids.option_d: The LORD is my strength and my song
kids.explanation: The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? (Psalm 27:1).

kids.question: What one thing does David desire more than anything else?
