kids.option_a: To dwell in the house of the LORD
kids.explanation: One thing have I desired of the LORD, that will I seek after; that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life (Psalm 27:4).

kids.question: What does David tell us to do at the end of Psalm 27?
kids.explanation: Wait on the LORD: be of good courage, and he shall strengthen thine heart: wait, I say, on the LORD (Psalm 27:14).

teens.question: What does David say he will not fear even if an army encamp against him in Psalm 27?
teens.option_b: I will trust in the LORD only
teens.explanation: Though an host should encamp against me, my heart shall not fear (Psalm 27:3).

teens.question: What does David say God will do for him in the time of trouble according to Psalm 27:5?
teens.option_a: He shall hide me in his pavilion: in the secret of his tabernacle shall he hide me
teens.option_b: He shall send his angel to protect me
teens.option_c: He shall scatter my enemies before me
teens.option_d: He shall give me a safe city
teens.explanation: In the time of trouble he shall hide me in his pavilion: in the secret of his tabernacle shall he hide me (Psalm 27:5).

teens.question: What does David seek — God's face — and what was he told?
teens.option_a: Seek ye my face; and my heart said, Thy face, LORD, will I seek
teens.option_b: Seek God in the sanctuary always
teens.option_c: Seek the LORD while he may be found
teens.option_d: Seek God in prayer each morning
teens.explanation: When thou saidst, Seek ye my face; my heart said unto thee, Thy face, LORD, will I seek (Psalm 27:8).

teens.question: What does David say his father and mother might do but what would God do instead?
teens.option_a: When my father and my mother forsake me, then the LORD will take me up
teens.option_b: My parents grow old but God grows not
teens.option_c: My parents sleep but God never sleeps
teens.option_d: My parents err but God never errs
teens.explanation: When my father and my mother forsake me, then the LORD will take me up (Psalm 27:10).

teens.question: What does David ask to behold in the house of the LORD in Psalm 27:4?
teens.explanation: To behold the beauty of the LORD, and to enquire in his temple (Psalm 27:4).

adults.question: What is the widely discussed structural debate about Psalm 27?
adults.option_a: Whether it was written by David or another psalmist
adults.option_b: Whether Psalm 27 is one psalm or two — vv.1-6 expressing confident trust and vv.7-14 expressing anxious lament — reflecting either one poet's experience or two separate compositions
adults.option_c: Whether it belongs in Book 1 or Book 2
adults.option_d: Whether it is Messianic or purely personal
adults.explanation: Scholars debate whether Psalm 27 is a unified psalm (trust flowing into crisis) or two compositions — the confident opening (1-6) and the anxious lament (7-14) — differing in tone.

adults.question: What is the theological weight of the opening declaration "The LORD is my light and my salvation" in Psalm 27:1?
adults.option_a: A general statement about God's attributes
adults.option_b: It combines two critical divine roles: light (guidance in darkness) and salvation (deliverance from danger) — making God the total answer to fear
adults.option_c: A liturgical greeting
adults.option_d: A metaphor for the sunrise
adults.explanation: The LORD is my light (guidance in darkness) and my salvation (deliverance from danger) (Psalm 27:1) — these two attributes together make fear impossible for the believer.

adults.question: What is the "one thing" David desires in Psalm 27:4, and why is the singularity of focus significant?
adults.option_a: David wanted only the temple built
adults.option_b: Singular focus on dwelling in God's presence above all other goods — expressing total devotion to communion with God as the supreme life-ambition
adults.option_c: David wanted to be king forever
adults.option_d: David wanted victory over all enemies
adults.explanation: One thing have I desired...that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life (Psalm 27:4) — singular focus on God's presence as life's supreme and only essential desire.

adults.question: What does "behold the beauty of the LORD" in Psalm 27:4 express about the nature of worship?
adults.option_a: Temple architecture is the object of admiration
adults.option_b: The goal of worship is the contemplation of God's own loveliness and character — noam (beauty/pleasantness) suggests God himself is the supreme delight
adults.option_c: Beauty refers to the ritual ceremonies
adults.option_d: David was an artist who appreciated aesthetics
adults.explanation: To behold the beauty (noam — pleasantness/loveliness) of the LORD (Psalm 27:4) — the goal of approaching God is not petitioning but gazing on his character with delight.

adults.question: What does "he shall hide me in his pavilion" in Psalm 27:5 evoke regarding divine protection?
adults.option_a: A specific room in the temple
adults.option_b: The sukkah (booth/pavilion) — evoking the Feast of Tabernacles, God's sheltering presence with his people in the wilderness
adults.option_c: A metaphor for sleep
adults.option_d: A military shelter
adults.explanation: In the time of trouble he shall hide me in his pavilion (sukkah) (Psalm 27:5) — the word evokes the feast of Tabernacles and God's wilderness-sheltering presence.

adults.question: What is the significance of David's claim that even his father and mother might forsake him, but the LORD would take him up in Psalm 27:10?
adults.option_b: It establishes God as a more reliable attachment figure than even the most fundamental human relationships — a radical declaration of divine faithfulness
adults.option_c: It predicts that David's parents would die soon
adults.option_d: It is a hypothetical with no real meaning
adults.explanation: When my father and my mother forsake me, then the LORD will take me up (Psalm 27:10) — setting God's faithfulness above even the most reliable human bond.

adults.question: What does "to enquire in his temple" in Psalm 27:4 suggest about the relationship between worship and seeking divine guidance?
adults.option_a: Enquiry is done separately from worship
adults.option_b: The temple is both the place of adoration (beholding beauty) and enquiry (seeking God's will) — worship and discernment are inseparable activities
adults.option_c: Only priests could enquire in the temple
adults.option_d: Enquiry replaces praise in times of crisis
adults.explanation: To behold the beauty of the LORD, and to enquire in his temple (Psalm 27:4) — adoration and seeking God's direction are simultaneous activities of the worshipper.

adults.question: How does the doubled "Wait on the LORD" in Psalm 27:14 function as the psalm's conclusion?
adults.option_a: It is a liturgical formula added by an editor
adults.option_b: The repetition is a rhetorical intensification — waiting on God is not passive resignation but courageous, active trust that strengthens the heart
adults.option_c: It shows David lost confidence
adults.option_d: It is addressed to future readers only
adults.explanation: Wait on the LORD: be of good courage, and he shall strengthen thine heart: wait, I say, on the LORD (Psalm 27:14) — the doubled imperative is emphatic, urging active, courageous expectation.

kids.question: What does David call God in his cry at the start of Psalm 28?
kids.explanation: Unto thee will I cry, O LORD my rock; be not silent to me (Psalm 28:1).

kids.question: What does David say the LORD is and who does he trust in Psalm 28?
kids.option_a: My strength and my shield; my heart trusted in him
kids.option_b: My rock and my fortress; I trust in him always
kids.option_c: My shepherd and my guide; I follow him always
kids.option_d: My light and my salvation; I shall not fear
kids.explanation: The LORD is my strength and my shield; my heart trusted in him, and I am helped (Psalm 28:7).

kids.question: What does David say the LORD is to his anointed in Psalm 28?
kids.option_a: The saving strength of his anointed
kids.explanation: Save thy people, and bless thine inheritance: feed them also, and lift them up for ever. The LORD is their strength, and he is the saving strength of his anointed (Psalm 28:8-9).

teens.question: What does David fear will happen if God is silent to him in Psalm 28?
teens.option_a: I become like them that go down into the pit
teens.option_d: My prayers will go unanswered forever
teens.explanation: Be not silent to me: lest, if thou be silent to me, I become like them that go down into the pit (Psalm 28:1).

teens.question: Toward what does David lift his hands in Psalm 28?
teens.explanation: Hear the voice of my supplications, when I cry unto thee, when I lift up my hands toward thy holy oracle (Psalm 28:2).

teens.question: What do the wicked do in Psalm 28:3 that makes them dangerous?
teens.option_a: They speak peace to their neighbours but mischief is in their hearts
teens.option_b: They set traps for the innocent
teens.option_d: They oppress the poor with violence
teens.explanation: Which speak peace to their neighbours, but mischief is in their hearts (Psalm 28:3).

teens.question: What does David ask God to give the wicked according to their deeds in Psalm 28:4?
teens.option_a: Give them according to their deeds and according to the wickedness of their endeavours
teens.option_b: Destroy them with fire from heaven
teens.option_c: Scatter them to the four winds
teens.explanation: Give them according to their deeds, and according to the wickedness of their endeavours (Psalm 28:4).

teens.question: What does David's heart do and what does his song break into in Psalm 28:7?
teens.option_a: My heart trusted in him and I am helped: therefore my heart greatly rejoiceth and with my song will I praise him
teens.option_b: My heart is glad and my tongue sings for joy
teens.option_c: My heart is satisfied and I will never be moved
teens.option_d: My heart overflows and I will shout for joy
teens.explanation: My heart trusted in him, and I am helped: therefore my heart greatly rejoiceth; and with my song will I praise him (Psalm 28:7).

adults.question: What is the structural movement of Psalm 28 from petition to praise?
adults.option_a: The psalm remains in petition throughout
adults.option_b: Verses 1-5 are intense petition; verses 6-9 are sudden praise — the shift indicates the psalmist's prayer has been heard (or received as heard by faith)
adults.option_c: The psalm is entirely praise
adults.option_d: The praise is placed at the beginning
adults.explanation: Psalm 28 moves from urgent petition (vv.1-5) to sudden praise (vv.6-9): "Blessed be the LORD, because he hath heard the voice of my supplications" (Psalm 28:6).

adults.question: What does "the holy oracle" (devir) in Psalm 28:2 refer to?
adults.option_b: The inner sanctuary of the temple — the Holy of Holies where God's presence dwelt above the ark
adults.option_c: A special prayer room
adults.option_d: The outer court of the temple
adults.explanation: I lift up my hands toward thy holy oracle (Psalm 28:2) — devir refers to the inner sanctuary, the Holy of Holies, the most holy chamber of the temple.

adults.question: Why does David distinguish between himself and "the workers of iniquity" who speak peace while planning mischief in Psalm 28:3?
adults.option_b: He is establishing his innocence as part of his appeal — he has not acted with duplicity as they have, making his cry for deliverance just
adults.option_c: He is warning others about false friends
adults.option_d: He is making a sociological observation
adults.explanation: Draw me not away with the wicked...which speak peace to their neighbours, but mischief is in their hearts (Psalm 28:3) — David's integrity contrasts with their duplicity, forming his claim to divine help.

adults.question: What does "feed them also, and lift them up for ever" in Psalm 28:9 reveal about David's understanding of his royal function?
adults.option_a: David sees himself as a farmer
adults.option_b: The king's role is pastoral — to feed (shepherd) and lift up (carry) God's people — reflecting the shepherd-king theology of the ancient Near East
adults.option_c: David is asking God to feed his army
adults.option_d: The verse is about agricultural abundance
adults.explanation: Feed them also, and lift them up for ever (Psalm 28:9) — the language is pastoral; the king's divinely appointed role is to shepherd and sustain God's flock.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of "Blessed be the LORD, because he hath heard the voice of my supplications" in Psalm 28:6?
adults.option_a: It is premature thanksgiving before answered prayer
adults.option_b: It is a confident declaration of faith — the psalmist blesses God for answered prayer, expressing certainty that the petition has been received
adults.option_c: It is a liturgical formula unrelated to David's specific prayer
adults.option_d: It contradicts the preceding lament
adults.explanation: Blessed be the LORD, because he hath heard the voice of my supplications (Psalm 28:6) — this sudden shift to blessing indicates the psalmist's confident assurance that God has heard.

adults.question: What does the juxtaposition of "saving strength of his anointed" and "bless thine inheritance" in Psalm 28:8-9 connect?
adults.option_b: The king (anointed) and the people (inheritance) — both are objects of God's saving care, showing that the king's welfare and the people's welfare are inseparable
adults.option_c: The temple and the palace
adults.option_d: Prayer and sacrifice
adults.explanation: The LORD is the saving strength of his anointed (king) — save thy people and bless thine inheritance (people) (Psalm 28:8-9) — king and people together are God's concern.

adults.question: What is the danger of God being "silent" in Psalm 28:1, and what does this reveal about the nature of divine communication?
adults.option_a: God's silence means he does not exist
adults.option_b: Divine silence is equivalent to abandonment — God's active response to prayer is constitutive of the relationship; his silence disrupts the covenantal bond
adults.option_c: Silence means God is pleased
adults.option_d: Silence means David needs to pray louder
adults.explanation: Be not silent to me: lest I become like them that go down into the pit (Psalm 28:1) — God's silence would be indistinguishable from absence, destroying the covenantal relationship.

adults.question: How does the retribution language of Psalm 28:4 ("give them according to their deeds") relate to the New Testament's principle of divine justice?
adults.option_a: It contradicts the New Testament's grace
adults.option_b: It reflects the biblical principle that God repays according to works (Romans 2:6), a principle consistent with both Testaments as it pertains to the final judgment
adults.option_c: It shows the Old Testament is inferior
adults.option_d: It applies only to Israel's enemies
adults.explanation: Give them according to their deeds (Psalm 28:4) — consistent with Romans 2:6: "God will render to every man according to his deeds" — the principle of moral accountability spans both Testaments.

kids.question: What does Psalm 29 call the voice of the LORD?
kids.explanation: The voice of the LORD is powerful; the voice of the LORD is full of majesty (Psalm 29:4).

kids.question: How many times is "the voice of the LORD" mentioned in Psalm 29?
kids.explanation: "The voice of the LORD" appears seven times in Psalm 29 (vv.3-9), representing the completeness of God's powerful voice.

kids.question: What does the LORD sit upon in Psalm 29?
kids.explanation: The LORD sitteth upon the flood; yea, the LORD sitteth King for ever (Psalm 29:10).

teens.question: Who is called to give glory and strength to the LORD in Psalm 29:1?
teens.explanation: Give unto the LORD, O ye mighty, give unto the LORD glory and strength (Psalm 29:1).

teens.question: What does the voice of the LORD do to the cedars of Lebanon in Psalm 29?
teens.option_a: It breaketh the cedars of Lebanon
teens.option_b: It bends the cedars of Lebanon
teens.option_c: It topples the cedars of Lebanon
teens.option_d: It fills the cedars of Lebanon
teens.explanation: The voice of the LORD breaketh the cedars; yea, the LORD breaketh the cedars of Lebanon (Psalm 29:5).

teens.question: What do the inhabitants of the temple say in Psalm 29:9?
teens.explanation: And in his temple doth every one speak of his glory (Psalm 29:9).

teens.question: What does the LORD give to his people in Psalm 29:11?
teens.explanation: The LORD will give strength unto his people; the LORD will bless his people with peace (Psalm 29:11).

teens.question: What does the voice of the LORD make the wilderness do in Psalm 29?
teens.option_a: The voice of the LORD shaketh the wilderness of Kadesh
teens.option_b: The voice of the LORD fills the wilderness
teens.option_c: The voice of the LORD enlightens the wilderness
teens.option_d: The voice of the LORD waters the wilderness
teens.explanation: The voice of the LORD shaketh the wilderness; the LORD shaketh the wilderness of Kadesh (Psalm 29:8).

adults.question: Psalm 29 has what relationship to ancient Canaanite Baal hymns, and what is the theological significance?
adults.option_a: It is completely original with no parallels
adults.option_b: It uses similar storm-theophany imagery but attributes all power to YHWH, not Baal — a polemic claiming that what Canaanite mythology attributed to Baal belongs exclusively to Israel's God
adults.option_c: It was written in response to Canaanite worship to condemn Israel
adults.option_d: It borrows Canaanite mythology uncritically
adults.explanation: Psalm 29 uses storm imagery similar to Canaanite Baal hymns but deliberately attributes all power to YHWH — a polemic demythologizing Baal and asserting YHWH's sole sovereignty.

adults.question: What is the significance of the voice of the LORD appearing exactly seven times in Psalm 29?
adults.option_a: It is a coincidence of poetic style
adults.option_b: Seven represents completeness in Hebrew — God's voice completely pervades creation, exercising total sovereign power over all natural forces
adults.option_c: Seven was a sacred number in Canaan
adults.option_d: Seven refers to the seven days of creation
adults.explanation: The sevenfold "voice of the LORD" (Psalm 29:3-9) — seven being the number of completeness — expresses the total, all-pervading power of God's word through creation.

adults.question: What does the command to "give unto the LORD glory and strength" to the "mighty" (bene Elim) in Psalm 29:1 imply?
adults.option_b: The heavenly court — "sons of the divine beings/gods" (angelic beings) — are commanded to worship YHWH, asserting his supremacy over all divine beings
adults.option_c: The priesthood is addressed
adults.option_d: The nations are commanded to convert
adults.explanation: Give unto the LORD, O ye mighty (bene Elim — sons of gods/divine beings) (Psalm 29:1) — the heavenly court of divine beings is summoned to acknowledge YHWH's supreme glory.

adults.question: What does the LORD's sitting "upon the flood" (mabbul) in Psalm 29:10 specifically recall?
adults.option_b: The Noahic flood (mabbul is the specific Hebrew word used only for Noah's flood) — asserting God's eternal sovereignty over the most catastrophic natural disaster in history
adults.option_c: The Jordan crossing
adults.option_d: A general reference to storm waters
adults.explanation: The LORD sitteth upon the flood (mabbul) (Psalm 29:10) — mabbul is the specific word for Noah's catastrophic flood (Genesis 6-8), asserting YHWH's eternal throne above even the greatest cosmic disaster.

adults.question: How does Psalm 29 move from cosmic theophany to pastoral blessing, and what does this reveal about God's character?
adults.option_a: The pastoral blessing is unconnected to the theophany
adults.option_b: The same God whose voice shatters cedars and shakes the wilderness gives strength and peace to his people — cosmic power channeled into tender pastoral care
adults.option_c: The blessing is for the angels only
adults.option_d: The cosmic events are metaphors for political events
adults.explanation: The voice that breaks cedars and shakes the wilderness (vv.5-8) is the same LORD who gives strength and peace to his people (v.11) — cosmic majesty and pastoral tenderness united.

adults.question: What does "in his temple doth every one speak of his glory" in Psalm 29:9 reveal about the purpose of corporate worship?
adults.option_b: The entire created order's response to God's power (vv.3-9) culminates in the temple congregation's declaration of glory — worship is the appropriate human response to divine theophany
adults.option_c: The temple is more holy than creation
adults.option_d: Only the clergy declare God's glory
adults.explanation: In his temple doth every one speak of his glory (Psalm 29:9) — the temple congregation's worship is the human echo of creation's testimony to YHWH's power throughout the psalm.

adults.question: What is the theological structure of Psalm 29: address (vv.1-2), theophany (vv.3-9), enthronement (v.10), and blessing (v.11)?
adults.option_b: The psalm moves from summons to worship → God's voice in storm → God enthroned → God's blessing: a complete theological narrative from creation's praise to the covenant community's peace
adults.option_c: The structure is a lament
adults.option_d: The structure belongs to wisdom literature
adults.explanation: Psalm 29 follows: summons to heavenly court (vv.1-2) → sevenfold theophany (vv.3-9) → eternal enthronement (v.10) → blessing of God's people (v.11) — a complete theological arc.

adults.question: How does the closing blessing "the LORD will bless his people with peace" (shalom) in Psalm 29:11 reframe all the thunderous power in the psalm?
adults.option_b: It reveals that God's terrifying cosmic power exists for the purpose of shalom — the welfare, wholeness, and flourishing of his covenant people
adults.option_c: Peace is an afterthought to power
adults.option_d: The blessing is for future generations only
adults.explanation: The LORD will bless his people with peace (Psalm 29:11) — all the thunderous power of vv.3-10 is channeled toward shalom (wholeness/flourishing) for the covenant people.

kids.question: What does Psalm 30 say about weeping and joy?
kids.option_a: Weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning
kids.option_c: Weeping is only for a moment
kids.explanation: Weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning (Psalm 30:5).

kids.question: What did God do for David when his enemies could not rejoice over him?
kids.option_b: Thou hast saved me from danger
kids.explanation: I will extol thee, O LORD; for thou hast lifted me up, and hast not made my foes to rejoice over me (Psalm 30:1).

kids.question: What does God turn into dancing in Psalm 30?
kids.explanation: Thou hast turned for me my mourning into dancing (Psalm 30:11).

teens.question: What is the superscription of Psalm 30 about?
teens.option_a: A Song at the dedication of the house of David
teens.option_b: A prayer in time of sickness
teens.option_c: A song of victory after battle
teens.option_d: A thanksgiving for deliverance from enemies
teens.explanation: The superscription reads: "A Psalm and Song at the dedication of the house of David."

teens.question: What does David say God prevented him from when he cried to him in Psalm 30?
teens.option_a: Thou hast healed me and not let my foes rejoice over me
teens.option_b: Thou hast kept me from falling into the pit
teens.option_c: Thou hast saved me from the sword of mine enemies
teens.option_d: Thou hast protected me from the fire of my enemies
teens.explanation: O LORD my God, I cried unto thee, and thou hast healed me. O LORD, thou hast brought up my soul from the grave: thou hast kept me alive (Psalm 30:2-3).

teens.question: How long does God's anger last compared to his favour according to Psalm 30:5?
teens.option_a: His anger is but for a moment; in his favour is life
teens.option_b: His anger lasts one year; his favour lasts forever
teens.option_c: His anger endures a day; his favour endures a week
teens.option_d: His anger passes in a night; his favour shines in the morning
teens.explanation: For his anger endureth but a moment; in his favour is life: weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning (Psalm 30:5).

teens.question: What did David say in his prosperity that made him overconfident in Psalm 30?
teens.option_d: I am the greatest of kings
teens.explanation: And in my prosperity I said, I shall never be moved (Psalm 30:6).

teens.question: What does David say God has clothed him with instead of sackcloth in Psalm 30:11?
teens.explanation: Thou hast turned for me my mourning into dancing: thou hast put off my sackcloth, and girded me with gladness (Psalm 30:11).

adults.question: What is the superscription "A Psalm and Song at the dedication of the house of David" and how has this been interpreted historically?
adults.option_a: The psalm was written by Solomon for the temple
adults.option_b: It may refer to dedication of David's palace (2 Samuel 5:11), or was later adapted for use at Hanukkah — the re-dedication of the temple after Maccabean victory
adults.option_c: It refers to the ark being brought to Zion
adults.option_d: It was written for the dedication of a private home
adults.explanation: The superscription (Psalm 30:1) has been interpreted as referring to David's house dedication (2 Samuel 5) or adapted for Hanukkah (temple re-dedication), hence its use in Jewish liturgy for Hanukkah.

adults.question: What does "thou hast brought up my soul from the grave" (she'ol) in Psalm 30:3 indicate about the nature of the crisis?
adults.option_b: A near-death illness from which David was miraculously recovered — she'ol describes the extreme danger from which God rescued him
adults.option_c: A spiritual descent into sin
adults.option_d: A metaphor for deep sadness
adults.explanation: Thou hast brought up my soul from the grave (she'ol) (Psalm 30:3) — the language of descent into she'ol indicates a near-fatal illness from which God gave recovery.

adults.question: What is the theological danger exposed in David's confession "in my prosperity I said, I shall never be moved" in Psalm 30:6?
adults.option_b: Prosperity can produce a self-reliant independence from God — the very stability God provides can become a source of forgetting God
adults.option_c: David was being falsely modest
adults.option_d: David was expressing appropriate confidence
adults.explanation: In my prosperity I said, I shall never be moved (Psalm 30:6) — prosperity bred self-sufficiency; when God hid his face David was suddenly troubled, exposing the fragility of human confidence.

adults.question: What argument does David use in Psalm 30:9 to motivate God to heal him, similar to Psalm 6:5?
adults.option_b: What profit is there in my blood if I go down to the pit? The dead cannot praise God — his life serves God's glory
adults.option_c: His enemies will mock God if he dies
adults.option_d: His family will suffer without him
adults.explanation: What profit is there in my blood, when I go down to the pit? Shall the dust praise thee? shall it declare thy truth? (Psalm 30:9) — David argues his living praise serves God's purposes (cf. Psalm 6:5).

adults.question: What is the significance of the contrast between "a moment" and "a night" in Psalm 30:5 regarding God's anger and favour?
adults.option_b: God's anger is momentary and temporary; his favour constitutes life itself — the fundamental asymmetry shows God's love is his dominant disposition toward his people
adults.option_c: Anger and favour are equal in duration
adults.option_d: This is a general observation about human emotions
adults.explanation: His anger endureth but a moment; in his favour is life (Psalm 30:5) — anger is quantified as a moment; favour is equated with life itself — a massive asymmetry revealing God's character.

adults.question: What does the transformation from sackcloth to gladness in Psalm 30:11 symbolize in the context of ancient mourning customs?
adults.option_b: Sackcloth was the garment of grief and penitence; gladness is the eschatological restoration — God reverses every sign of mourning and replaces it with celebration
adults.option_c: A priestly investiture ceremony
adults.option_d: David is describing a literal event of changing clothes
adults.explanation: Thou hast put off my sackcloth, and girded me with gladness (Psalm 30:11) — sackcloth represented mourning; God's reversal of sackcloth to gladness is a complete transformation of status.

adults.question: How does "weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning" in Psalm 30:5 function as a theodicy?
adults.option_a: It denies that suffering is real
adults.option_b: It does not minimize suffering (weeping is real) but asserts its temporality — God's redemptive morning is guaranteed to follow every night of tears
adults.option_c: It promises immediate relief
adults.option_d: It teaches that joy always follows weeping the very next morning
adults.explanation: Weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning (Psalm 30:5) — a theodicy that affirms suffering's reality while insisting on its temporal limit within God's redemptive purposes.

adults.question: What does the final purpose clause "to the end that my glory may sing praise to thee, and not be silent" in Psalm 30:12 reveal about the ultimate purpose of deliverance?
adults.option_a: God delivers for David's personal benefit
adults.option_b: God's deliverance has as its goal the praise of the delivered — deliverance creates worshippers, and worship is the ultimate purpose of redemption
adults.option_c: God delivers to prove his power to enemies
adults.option_d: God delivers to show David's worthiness
adults.explanation: To the end that my glory may sing praise to thee, and not be silent (Psalm 30:12) — the purpose of divine rescue is the creation of praise: deliverance exists for worship.

kids.question: Where did David ask God to lead him when his heart was overwhelmed?
kids.explanation: David prayed, "Lead me to the rock that is higher than I" (Psalm 61:2).

kids.question: What did David want to do in God's tabernacle forever?
kids.explanation: David said, "I will abide in thy tabernacle for ever" (Psalm 61:4).

kids.question: What did David say he would do daily in the psalm?
kids.explanation: David said, "So will I sing praise unto thy name for ever, that I may daily perform my vows" (Psalm 61:8).

teens.question: From where did David cry unto God in Psalm 61?
teens.option_b: From the end of the earth
teens.explanation: David cried, "From the end of the earth will I cry unto thee, when my heart is overwhelmed" (Psalm 61:2).

teens.question: What two things did David say God had been to him?
teens.explanation: David said God had been "a shelter for me, and a strong tower from the enemy" (Psalm 61:3).

teens.question: What did David say he trusted in regarding the king in Psalm 61?
teens.option_a: That the king would conquer all enemies
teens.option_b: That God would prolong the king's life
teens.option_c: That the king would build a great temple
teens.option_d: That the king would defeat David's enemies
teens.explanation: David prayed that God would prolong the king's life and that "his years as many generations" (Psalm 61:6).

teens.question: What two things did David ask to be sent to preserve the king?
teens.explanation: David prayed, "O prepare mercy and truth, which may preserve him" (Psalm 61:7).

teens.question: What musical term heads Psalm 61, indicating it was to be used with what instrument?
teens.explanation: Psalm 61 is headed "To the chief Musician upon Neginah" — a stringed instrument (Psalm 61 superscription).

adults.question: What Hebrew word rendered "overwhelmed" in Psalm 61:2 conveys the sense of fainting or being covered over?
adults.option_b: Ataph
adults.option_c: Nephesh
adults.option_d: Shachah
adults.explanation: The word "ataph" (overwhelmed) in Psalm 61:2 means to be faint, covered, or feeble — David feels utterly spent.

adults.question: What does David's phrase "the rock that is higher than I" theologially convey?
adults.option_b: God's salvation is beyond David's own ability to reach it
adults.option_c: David wanted to climb a literal rock
adults.option_d: God's throne is above all creation
adults.explanation: The phrase "lead me to the rock that is higher than I" (Psalm 61:2) expresses that God's help transcends human ability.

adults.question: What does David's desire to "abide in thy tabernacle for ever" (Psalm 61:4) reflect about his deepest longing?
adults.option_b: An unbroken dwelling in God's presence as the highest good
adults.option_c: A desire to live in Jerusalem
adults.option_d: A vow to never leave the city
adults.explanation: David's longing to abide in God's tabernacle forever (Psalm 61:4) reflects that God's presence is his supreme joy.

adults.question: The phrase "under the shadow of thy wings" in Psalm 61:4 is a recurring OT image. What does it primarily symbolize?
adults.option_b: Divine protection and sheltering care
adults.option_c: The wings of cherubim on the ark
adults.option_d: The eagle imagery of Deuteronomy
adults.explanation: The shadow of God's wings (Psalm 61:4) is a tender image of divine shelter and protection, used also in Psalm 17:8 and 91:4.

adults.question: How does Psalm 61:6-7 function as a Messianic or royal prayer?
adults.option_a: It prophesies the birth of a king in Bethlehem
adults.option_b: It intercedes for the ideal king whose reign is perpetual, ultimately fulfilled in Christ
adults.option_c: It describes David's own coronation
adults.option_d: It predicts the return of Solomon
adults.explanation: The prayer for the king's endless life and mercy (Psalm 61:6-7) points beyond any earthly king to the eternal reign of Christ.

adults.question: What vow does David make at the close of Psalm 61?
adults.option_a: To offer a thousand burnt offerings
adults.option_b: To daily perform his vows and sing praise to God's name forever
adults.option_c: To never leave Jerusalem
adults.option_d: To build a house for God
adults.explanation: David concludes, "So will I sing praise unto thy name for ever, that I may daily perform my vows" (Psalm 61:8).

adults.question: What attribute of God is emphasized by the pairing of "mercy and truth" in Psalm 61:7?
adults.option_b: God's covenant faithfulness and reliable love
adults.option_c: God's omniscience and justice
adults.option_d: God's creativity and wisdom
adults.explanation: Mercy (hesed — covenant love) and truth (emet — faithfulness) together describe God's reliable covenant character (Psalm 61:7).

adults.question: What does the heading "upon Neginah" suggest about the original liturgical use of Psalm 61?
adults.option_b: It was performed with a single stringed instrument, likely the lute or lyre
adults.option_c: It was a whispered prayer
adults.option_d: It was chanted by a choir
adults.explanation: Neginah refers to a stringed instrument; the psalm was composed for musical accompaniment in Israel's worship (Psalm 61 superscription).

kids.question: What did David say his soul did toward God in Psalm 62?
kids.explanation: David said, "My soul waiteth upon God: from him cometh my salvation" (Psalm 62:1).

kids.question: What did David call God in Psalm 62 — his rock and his what?
kids.explanation: David called God "my rock and my salvation" (Psalm 62:2).

kids.question: Who does Psalm 62 say power belongs to?
kids.explanation: David declared, "Power belongeth unto God" (Psalm 62:11).

teens.question: How does David describe trusting in men of low and high degree in Psalm 62?
teens.option_a: They are trustworthy if chosen by God
teens.option_b: They are altogether vanity and lighter than a breath
teens.option_c: They are good helpers in times of need
teens.option_d: They can be trusted if they are righteous
teens.explanation: David wrote that men of low and high degree are "altogether vanity... lighter than vanity" (Psalm 62:9).

teens.question: What did David say not to trust in, specifically mentioning robbery?
teens.option_b: Oppression and robbery — and riches that increase
teens.explanation: David warned, "Trust not in oppression, and become not vain in robbery: if riches increase, set not your heart upon them" (Psalm 62:10).

teens.question: How many times does the phrase "only" (or "only he") appear as a refrain in Psalm 62, emphasizing exclusive trust in God?
teens.explanation: The word "only" (Hebrew: ak) appears as a refrain in Psalm 62:1, 2, 4, 5, 6 — about three key refrains stressing sole dependence on God.

teens.question: To what does David compare his enemies' assault on him in Psalm 62:3?
teens.option_b: A leaning wall and a tottering fence
teens.explanation: David asks, "How long will ye imagine mischief against a man? ye shall be slain all of you: as a bowing wall shall ye be, and as a tottering fence" (Psalm 62:3).

teens.question: What two things does David say God renders to every man in Psalm 62:12?
teens.option_b: Mercy and salvation according to his work
teens.explanation: David wrote, "Also unto thee, O Lord, belongeth mercy: for thou renderest to every man according to his work" (Psalm 62:12).

adults.question: What Hebrew term is translated "truly" or "only" (ak) and serves as the structural refrain of Psalm 62, appearing in verses 1, 2, 4, 5, and 6?
adults.option_b: Ak
adults.option_c: Higgaion
adults.option_d: Maskil
adults.explanation: The particle ak (only/surely/truly) anchors each stanza of Psalm 62, directing the reader's sole trust to God.

adults.question: What does "my soul waiteth upon God" (Psalm 62:1) — the Hebrew "dummiyyah" — literally mean?
adults.option_b: Silent rest or stillness before God
adults.option_c: Loud praise
adults.option_d: Patient endurance in suffering
adults.explanation: Dummiyyah means silence or stillness; "my soul waiteth in silence upon God" (Psalm 62:1) speaks of calm, wordless trust.

adults.question: What does David's metaphor of God as a "rock" and "fortress" (Psalm 62:2, 6-7) emphasize?
adults.option_b: God's immovable stability and protection in contrast to flimsy human support
adults.option_c: The temple's stone construction
adults.option_d: The mountains of Israel
adults.explanation: The rock and fortress imagery (Psalm 62:2, 6-7) contrasts God's absolute stability with the vanity of human help.

adults.question: How does Psalm 62:9 use a balance/scale image to make its point about humanity?
adults.option_a: It says the righteous outweigh the wicked
adults.option_b: It says all men laid in a balance are lighter than vanity
adults.option_c: It says God weighs human hearts
adults.option_d: It says riches weigh down the soul
adults.explanation: Psalm 62:9 says men of low and high degree "laid in the balance...are altogether lighter than vanity."

adults.question: What do the two attributes named in Psalm 62:11-12 — "power" and "mercy" — reveal about God's character?
adults.option_a: That God is both distant and near
adults.option_b: That God's omnipotence is exercised through covenant love, not arbitrary force
adults.option_c: That God rewards the powerful with mercy
adults.option_d: That power and mercy compete in God
adults.explanation: Psalm 62:11-12 pairs God's power with mercy, showing his might is always governed by his covenant love.

adults.question: What does "God hath spoken once; twice have I heard this" (Psalm 62:11) illustrate as a literary device?
adults.option_a: God spoke audibly twice to David
adults.option_b: A numerical graduation device (x, x+1) emphasizing the certainty of what was said
adults.option_c: David had two prophetic dreams
adults.option_d: God repeats himself for emphasis only
adults.explanation: The "once...twice" formula (Psalm 62:11) is a Hebrew literary device (cf. Proverbs 6:16) stressing the absolute certainty of the truth.

adults.question: What does David mean by "pour out your heart before him" (Psalm 62:8)?
adults.option_b: Express every burden, emotion, and need openly to God in prayer
adults.option_c: Weep publicly in the temple
adults.option_d: Confess sins to the priest
adults.explanation: Pouring out the heart before God (Psalm 62:8) is an image of complete, uninhibited self-disclosure in prayer (cf. 1 Samuel 1:15).

adults.question: How does Jeduthun's name in the heading of Psalm 62 connect to Israel's worship structure?
adults.option_a: Jeduthun was a king of Israel
adults.option_b: Jeduthun was one of David's three chief musicians appointed for temple worship
adults.option_c: Jeduthun was a scribe who collected psalms
adults.option_d: Jeduthun was a prophet who interpreted psalms
adults.explanation: Jeduthun (1 Chronicles 16:41-42) was one of David's three chief worship leaders; Psalm 62 was committed to his care for Israel's liturgy.

kids.question: Where was David when he wrote Psalm 63?
kids.option_d: In a cave by the sea
kids.explanation: The superscription reads, "A Psalm of David, when he was in the wilderness of Judah" (Psalm 63 superscription).

kids.question: What did David say his soul did for God like a thirsty body?
kids.explanation: David said, "My soul thirsteth for thee, my flesh longeth for thee in a dry and thirsty land" (Psalm 63:1).

kids.question: What did David say he would do while lying on his bed at night?
kids.option_b: Remember God and meditate on him
kids.explanation: David said, "When I remember thee upon my bed, and meditate on thee in the night watches" (Psalm 63:6).

teens.question: What does David say is better than life in Psalm 63:3?
teens.explanation: David declared, "Because thy lovingkindness is better than life, my lips shall praise thee" (Psalm 63:3).

teens.question: How does David describe his praise — what posture does he mention?
teens.option_b: Lifting up his hands in God's name
teens.explanation: David wrote, "Thus will I bless thee while I live: I will lift up my hands in thy name" (Psalm 63:4).

teens.question: What image does David use to describe his soul's satisfaction in God?
teens.explanation: David said, "My soul shall be satisfied as with marrow and fatness; and my mouth shall praise thee with joyful lips" (Psalm 63:5).

teens.question: What does David say about those who seek his soul to destroy it?
teens.option_b: They will go into the lower parts of the earth and fall by the sword
teens.option_c: They will be scattered like chaff
teens.option_d: They will be turned back in shame
teens.explanation: David said his enemies "shall go into the lower parts of the earth... they shall fall by the sword" (Psalm 63:9-10).

teens.question: What does David call himself in Psalm 63:1 — using what divine title for God?
teens.option_b: O God, thou art my God
teens.explanation: David opens with "O God, thou art my God; early will I seek thee" (Psalm 63:1).

adults.question: What does the Hebrew word "shachar" (translated "early will I seek thee" in Psalm 63:1) literally convey?
adults.option_b: Diligently seeking at dawn, implying earnest and urgent pursuit
adults.option_c: Searching in darkness
adults.option_d: Seeking with the whole congregation
adults.explanation: Shachar means to seek at dawn or diligently; "early will I seek thee" (Psalm 63:1) conveys urgent, first-priority devotion.

adults.question: What is the theological weight of David's assertion that lovingkindness is "better than life" (Psalm 63:3)?
adults.option_a: David preferred death to life without God
adults.option_b: God's covenant love (hesed) surpasses physical existence as the highest good
adults.option_c: Life in the wilderness was miserable
adults.option_d: David was suicidal and longed for heaven
adults.explanation: To declare lovingkindness better than life (Psalm 63:3) places God's hesed above the most precious earthly possession — life itself.

adults.question: The phrase "in the shadow of thy wings" (Psalm 63:7) echoes what broader OT theological theme?
adults.option_a: The wings of seraphim in Isaiah
adults.option_b: God's maternal sheltering of his people (cf. Deuteronomy 32:11; Ruth 2:12; Psalm 91:4)
adults.option_c: The eagle as a symbol of Israel's military power
adults.option_d: The wings of the cherubim on the mercy seat
adults.explanation: The shadow of God's wings (Psalm 63:7) is a recurring image of tender divine protection, rooted in Deuteronomy 32:11 and Boaz's blessing in Ruth 2:12.

adults.question: How does David's night meditation (Psalm 63:6) reflect ancient Near Eastern spiritual practice?
adults.option_a: Night was considered the time of evil spirits
adults.option_b: The night watches were times of prayer and contemplation in Israel's worship calendar
adults.option_c: David could not sleep due to enemies
adults.option_d: Night was set aside for fasting not prayer
adults.explanation: Night watches were established periods of prayer and guard (cf. Psalm 119:148); David's night meditation (Psalm 63:6) shows his constant communion with God.

adults.question: What does David's description of his soul clinging to God — "my soul followeth hard after thee" (Psalm 63:8) — convey in Hebrew (dabaq)?
adults.option_b: An intense, tenacious cleaving to God like a marriage bond (cf. Genesis 2:24)
adults.option_c: A desperate search in confusion
adults.option_d: A ritual act of sacrifice
adults.explanation: Dabaq (cleave/cling) in Psalm 63:8 is the same word used in Genesis 2:24 for a husband cleaving to his wife — total covenant attachment.

adults.question: What does the fate of David's enemies — their mouths stopped (Psalm 63:11) — contrast with in the psalm's structure?
adults.option_b: David's own lips praising and swearing by God, contrasted with lying mouths stopped
adults.option_c: The silence of night meditation
adults.option_d: The king's wordless reign
adults.explanation: The stopped mouths of liars (Psalm 63:11) contrast pointedly with David's praising lips (Psalm 63:3, 5) — praise silences wickedness.

adults.question: What military or royal context likely underlies Psalm 63's composition in the wilderness?
adults.option_a: David's flight from Saul in the Judean desert
adults.option_b: David's flight from Absalom's rebellion when he crossed the wilderness east of Jerusalem
adults.option_c: David's forty years of wandering
adults.option_d: David's campaign against the Philistines
adults.explanation: The superscription and David's reference to "the king" (Psalm 63:11) suggest this was written during Absalom's rebellion, when David fled across the Judean wilderness.

adults.question: Psalm 63:3 has been called one of the boldest statements in the Psalter. What makes it theologically radical?
adults.option_a: It claims God is visible in the wilderness
adults.option_b: It elevates divine love above physical life, reversing the natural hierarchy of values
adults.option_c: It denies the value of temple worship
adults.option_d: It asserts that martyrdom is the highest good
adults.explanation: By placing lovingkindness above life itself (Psalm 63:3), David subordinates the most prized earthly good to God's covenantal love — a radical reordering of values.

kids.question: What did David ask God to preserve him from in Psalm 64?
kids.explanation: David prayed, "Preserve my life from fear of the enemy" (Psalm 64:1).

kids.question: What did David say God would shoot at the wicked?
kids.explanation: David declared, "God shall shoot at them with an arrow; suddenly shall they be wounded" (Psalm 64:7).

kids.question: What would all men do when they saw God's judgment on the wicked?
kids.option_a: They would be afraid and declare God's work
kids.explanation: David said all men "shall fear, and shall declare the work of God; for they shall wisely consider of his doing" (Psalm 64:9).

teens.question: How does David describe the secret plotting of the wicked in Psalm 64?
teens.option_a: They plot openly in the city gates
teens.option_b: They shoot in secret at the blameless man with bitter words as arrows
teens.option_c: They gather armies against the righteous
teens.option_d: They bribe judges against the innocent
teens.explanation: David said they "shoot in secret at the perfect: suddenly do they shoot at him, and fear not" (Psalm 64:4).

teens.question: What do the wicked say about their evil plots in Psalm 64:5?
teens.explanation: The wicked encouraged themselves and said, "Who shall see them?" (Psalm 64:5).

teens.question: What body part of the wicked does David say will cause their downfall?
teens.explanation: David said, "So they shall make their own tongue to fall upon themselves" (Psalm 64:8).

teens.question: What does David say the righteous will do in response to God's judgment in Psalm 64:10?
teens.option_a: They will rejoice in the LORD and trust in him
teens.option_b: They will weep for the wicked
teens.option_c: They will build altars of thanksgiving
teens.explanation: David wrote, "The righteous shall be glad in the LORD, and shall trust in him; and all the upright in heart shall glory" (Psalm 64:10).

teens.question: What does David compare the enemy's sharp and bitter words to in Psalm 64:3?
teens.option_a: A sharp sword and bitter poison
teens.option_b: Their tongue like a sword and their words like arrows
teens.option_c: A bitter root and a sharp thorn
teens.explanation: David said the enemies "whet their tongue like a sword, and bend their bows to shoot their arrows, even bitter words" (Psalm 64:3).

adults.question: What literary device dominates Psalm 64, and how does it create a reversal between the wicked and the righteous?
adults.option_a: Chiasm — the structure mirrors itself perfectly
adults.option_b: Poetic justice — the weapons (arrows/words) the wicked use against David are turned against them by God
adults.option_c: Acrostic — each verse begins with a Hebrew letter
adults.option_d: Anaphora — the same opening phrase repeats
adults.explanation: The poetic justice of Psalm 64 is that God shoots the wicked with an arrow (64:7), reversing their secret shooting of arrows (bitter words) at the righteous (64:3-4).

adults.question: What does David's request to be hidden from the "secret counsel of the wicked" (Psalm 64:2) imply about the threat he faces?
adults.option_b: An organized conspiracy of hidden enemies plotting his destruction
adults.option_c: A foreign invasion
adults.option_d: A legal dispute before judges
adults.explanation: The "secret counsel" and "insurrection of workers of iniquity" (Psalm 64:2) describe a covert conspiracy — likely David's political enemies.

adults.question: What is the theological principle illustrated when "their own tongue" causes the wicked to fall (Psalm 64:8)?
adults.option_a: Sin is always found out by witnesses
adults.option_b: God uses the very instruments of wickedness as instruments of judgment — self-defeating sin
adults.option_c: Speech is the most powerful human weapon
adults.option_d: God punishes the wicked by silencing them
adults.explanation: When their own tongue falls upon themselves (Psalm 64:8), the psalm illustrates how sin carries within itself the seeds of its own destruction.

adults.question: What does the phrase "they commune of laying snares privily" (Psalm 64:5) reveal about the psychology of the wicked?
adults.option_a: They are cowards who fear open confrontation
adults.option_b: They encourage one another in secret scheming, mistakenly believing they are hidden from God
adults.option_c: They work alone and do not collaborate
adults.option_d: They are ignorant of moral law
adults.explanation: Psalm 64:5 exposes the self-deception of the wicked: they commune secretly and ask "who shall see them?" — forgetting God's omniscience.

adults.question: What does "they search out iniquities; they accomplish a diligent search" (Psalm 64:6) emphasize?
adults.option_b: The wicked apply painstaking effort and ingenuity to sin — wickedness is their industry
adults.option_c: God searches out the iniquities of the wicked
adults.option_d: The righteous investigate wrongdoing
adults.explanation: Psalm 64:6 ironically credits the wicked with great diligence — "they accomplish a diligent search" — showing that sin is a deliberate, effortful enterprise.

adults.question: How does "suddenly shall they be wounded" (Psalm 64:7) contrast with the plotting tempo earlier in the psalm?
adults.option_a: It shows God is slow to anger
adults.option_b: The wicked's careful, prolonged scheming is answered by God's instantaneous decisive judgment
adults.option_c: The wicked wound suddenly so God wounds slowly
adults.option_d: It shows judgment comes in stages
adults.explanation: The wicked plot carefully and patiently (Psalm 64:2-6), but God's arrow strikes suddenly (Psalm 64:7) — divine judgment comes without warning at the appointed moment.

adults.question: What is the communal effect described in Psalm 64:9 when all men see God's judgment?
adults.option_b: A universal fear of God and declaration of his works — a witness to the nations
adults.option_c: A political revolution
adults.option_d: A revival only among the righteous
adults.explanation: When all men fear and declare God's work (Psalm 64:9), God's judgment on the wicked becomes a universal testimony to his power and justice.

adults.question: What does Psalm 64's movement from personal lament to universal praise (verse 10) model for believers?
adults.option_a: That prayers are always answered quickly
adults.option_b: That individual trust in God's justice leads to corporate rejoicing — personal faith has communal dimensions
adults.option_c: That the righteous must wait passively
adults.option_d: That praise replaces the need for prayer
adults.explanation: Psalm 64 moves from David's personal plea (64:1-2) to universal praise (64:9-10), modeling how faith in God's justice becomes shared worship.

kids.question: What did the psalmist say God crowns the year with in Psalm 65?
kids.explanation: The psalmist declared, "Thou crownest the year with thy goodness" (Psalm 65:11).

kids.question: Where does God dwell and receive praise according to Psalm 65:1?
kids.explanation: The psalmist says, "Praise waiteth for thee, O God, in Sion" (Psalm 65:1).

kids.question: What did God do to the seas and waves to show his power in Psalm 65?
kids.option_b: He stilled the noise of them
kids.option_d: He raised them to the mountains
kids.explanation: The psalmist said God "stilleth the noise of the seas, the noise of their waves" (Psalm 65:7).

teens.question: What does Psalm 65:2 say that allows all flesh to come to God?
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "O thou that hearest prayer, unto thee shall all flesh come" (Psalm 65:2).

teens.question: What does God do with the transgressions of his people according to Psalm 65:3?
teens.explanation: The psalmist wrote, "As for our transgressions, thou shalt purge them away" (Psalm 65:3).

teens.question: What blessing is given to the one whom God chooses and causes to approach him in Psalm 65:4?
teens.option_b: Satisfaction with the goodness of God's house and holy temple
teens.explanation: The psalmist wrote, "Blessed is the man whom thou choosest... that he may dwell in thy courts: we shall be satisfied with the goodness of thy house" (Psalm 65:4).

teens.question: What does Psalm 65 say God does to establish the mountains, showing his great power?
teens.option_b: He is girded with strength and sets fast the mountains
teens.option_c: He lifts them from the sea
teens.option_d: He causes them to tremble and bow
teens.explanation: The psalmist declared, "Which by his strength setteth fast the mountains; being girded with power" (Psalm 65:6).

teens.question: How does Psalm 65 describe the tracks of God's chariot — what drops from them?
teens.explanation: The psalmist wrote, "Thy paths drop fatness" (Psalm 65:11), describing God's providential abundance.

adults.question: What does "praise waiteth for thee" (Psalm 65:1) mean in the Hebrew, where the verb is related to "silence" (dumiyyah)?
adults.option_a: Praise is offered in loud song
adults.option_b: Praise is quietly expectant — a still, reverent waiting before God that is itself a form of worship
adults.option_c: Praise has been postponed until later
adults.option_d: Praise is offered only by the priests
adults.explanation: The Hebrew dumiyyah suggests silent, expectant praise (Psalm 65:1) — adoration that is calm and awestruck before God's greatness.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of "unto thee shall all flesh come" (Psalm 65:2) in the context of this psalm?
adults.option_a: It predicts the physical resurrection of all people
adults.option_b: It indicates that God's hearing of prayer is universally available — no nation is excluded from approaching him
adults.option_c: It refers only to Israel's pilgrimage feasts
adults.option_d: It means all flesh will be judged
adults.explanation: The universal scope of "all flesh" (Psalm 65:2) anticipates the Gentile inclusion in the NT — all peoples may approach God through prayer.

adults.question: How does Psalm 65:3 balance human sinfulness with divine grace?
adults.option_a: It denies that the people have sinned
adults.option_b: It acknowledges that iniquities prevail against us, yet God purges transgressions — grace overwhelms sin
adults.option_c: It teaches that sin is unavoidable and unpunishable
adults.option_d: It promises that obedient people will never sin
adults.explanation: Psalm 65:3 is honest: "iniquities prevail against me," yet God purges them — a frank acknowledgment of human sin met by divine atoning grace.

adults.question: What does "thou that art the confidence of all the ends of the earth, and of them that are afar off upon the sea" (Psalm 65:5) reveal about the scope of God's salvation?
adults.option_a: God's power is limited to Israel and its borders
adults.option_b: God is the hope and security of all nations and peoples everywhere — a universalist missional vision
adults.option_c: The sea represents chaos that God must restrain
adults.option_d: The ends of the earth are unreachable by prayer
adults.explanation: Psalm 65:5 declares God is the confidence of all earth's ends — a remarkable missional statement that anticipates the gospel reaching every nation.

adults.question: What does the agricultural imagery of Psalm 65:9-13 (watering the earth, softening it, blessing the grain, crowning the year) theologically affirm?
adults.option_a: Israel must perform rain rituals to ensure harvest
adults.option_b: God is the sovereign provider of all natural blessing — creation itself is sustained by his personal care
adults.option_c: The harvest is a reward for Israel's obedience only
adults.option_d: Nature operates independently of God
adults.explanation: The rich agricultural imagery of Psalm 65:9-13 affirms that God personally visits and waters the earth — all creation depends on his providential care.

adults.question: What does "the river of God, which is full of water" (Psalm 65:9) represent in the context of this psalm?
adults.option_b: The heavenly supply of rain — God's provision channel for the earth's fertility
adults.option_c: A river in the garden of Eden
adults.option_d: The waters above the firmament
adults.explanation: The river of God full of water (Psalm 65:9) is a poetic image of God's heavenly reservoir of rain that he uses to water and bless the earth.

adults.question: How does Psalm 65 move structurally from the personal/communal to the cosmic?
adults.option_a: It begins with creation and ends with personal prayer
adults.option_b: It moves from personal forgiveness and temple access (65:1-4) through God's cosmic power (65:5-8) to universal agricultural blessing (65:9-13)
adults.option_c: It moves from farming to temple worship
adults.option_d: The structure is random and does not progress
adults.explanation: Psalm 65 is structured in three movements: personal salvation (65:1-4), cosmic power (65:5-8), and providential provision in nature (65:9-13).

adults.question: What does the psalmist mean that the valleys "shout for joy, they also sing" (Psalm 65:13)?
adults.option_a: Literally, the grain fields make audible sounds
adults.option_b: Nature itself joins creation's praise — all of creation is personified as a worshipping community
adults.option_c: The people in the valleys sing harvest songs
adults.option_d: Angels sing in the valleys
adults.explanation: The singing valleys (Psalm 65:13) personify creation as a worshipping community, anticipating Romans 8:19-22 where creation waits for redemption.

kids.question: What does Psalm 66 tell all the earth to make unto God?
kids.explanation: The psalmist declared, "Make a joyful noise unto God, all ye lands" (Psalm 66:1).

kids.question: What did God do to the sea that the people walked through on dry ground?
kids.option_b: He turned it into dry land
kids.explanation: The psalmist recalled, "He turned the sea into dry land: they went through the flood on foot" (Psalm 66:6).

kids.question: What did the psalmist say God had not done — proving he heard prayer?
kids.option_b: Turned away from him or withheld his mercy
kids.explanation: The psalmist said God had "not turned away my prayer, nor his mercy from me" (Psalm 66:20).

teens.question: What does the psalmist invite people to do in Psalm 66:5?
teens.option_b: Come and see the works of God
teens.explanation: The psalmist called, "Come and see the works of God: he is terrible in his doing toward the children of men" (Psalm 66:5).

teens.question: What two extreme experiences does the psalmist say God brought his people through in Psalm 66:12?
teens.option_a: Famine and plenty, war and peace
teens.option_b: Fire and water, then into a wealthy place
teens.option_c: The desert and the sea, the valley and the mountain
teens.option_d: Captivity and freedom, hunger and feasting
teens.explanation: The psalmist declared, "Thou hast caused men to ride over our heads; we went through fire and through water: but thou broughtest us out into a wealthy place" (Psalm 66:12).

teens.question: What specific offering does the psalmist vow to bring to God's house in Psalm 66:13-15?
teens.option_b: Burnt offerings, rams, bullocks, and goats
teens.explanation: The psalmist vowed to bring "burnt offerings... bullocks, with rams; I will offer bullocks with goats" (Psalm 66:13-15).

teens.question: What condition does the psalmist say would prevent God from hearing prayer in Psalm 66:18?
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "If I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me" (Psalm 66:18).

teens.question: What does Psalm 66:3 say that God's enemies do because of his great power?
teens.option_d: They call on him for mercy
teens.explanation: The psalmist wrote, "Say unto God, How terrible art thou in thy works! through the greatness of thy power shall thine enemies submit themselves unto thee" (Psalm 66:3).

adults.question: What does "make a joyful noise" (Psalm 66:1) — the Hebrew rua — specifically connote beyond mere singing?
adults.option_b: A loud, triumphant shout associated with battle victory and royal acclamation
adults.option_c: A musical scale or melody
adults.option_d: A priestly chant
adults.explanation: Rua (Psalm 66:1) is a shout of triumph, the same word used for the battle cry and the acclamation of kings — it is joyful clamour before God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 66:6 allude to the Exodus and what does this say about God's ongoing identity for Israel?
adults.option_a: It is a vague reference to any past miracle
adults.option_b: It recalls the parting of the Red Sea (Exodus 14), establishing that God's saving acts define his identity for every generation
adults.option_c: It refers to Joshua crossing the Jordan
adults.option_d: It describes a future event
adults.explanation: Psalm 66:6 recalls God turning the sea to dry land — clearly echoing Exodus 14 — reminding every generation that the Exodus God is still their God.

adults.question: What does "the eyes of God observe the nations" (Psalm 66:7) assert about divine governance?
adults.option_a: God is passive and watches without acting
adults.option_b: God's omniscient watch over nations means he actively governs history and will not allow rebellion to go unchecked
adults.option_c: God watches only Israel
adults.option_d: The nations are unaware of God's observation
adults.explanation: Psalm 66:7 asserts divine sovereignty over world history: God's watchful eyes over nations means he governs, not merely observes.

adults.question: What is the theological purpose of the trials described in Psalm 66:10-12 — the refining, burden, and fire and water?
adults.option_a: God punishes his people for sin without purpose
adults.option_b: God tests and refines his people as silver is refined — trials are instruments of purification and preparation for blessing
adults.option_c: The trials are accidents of history
adults.option_d: The people brought trials upon themselves only
adults.explanation: Psalm 66:10 says God has "proved us... as silver is tried" — the fire and water of Psalm 66:12 are intentional purifying trials leading to a wealthy place.

adults.question: What is the distinction in Psalm 66:13-15 between vows made in distress and their fulfillment in prosperity?
adults.option_a: The psalmist forgot his vows in prosperity
adults.option_b: The psalmist faithfully fulfills in public worship what he promised in private crisis — integrity of covenant commitment
adults.option_c: Vows made in distress are not binding
adults.option_d: The offerings replace the verbal vows
adults.explanation: The psalmist vowed offerings in distress (Psalm 66:14) and now brings them (Psalm 66:13, 15) — a model of vow-keeping integrity (cf. Ecclesiastes 5:4-5).

adults.question: What does "I regard iniquity in my heart" (Psalm 66:18) teach about the prerequisites for effective prayer?
adults.option_b: Cherished unconfessed sin creates a barrier between the worshipper and God's hearing
adults.option_c: Prayer is effective regardless of the heart's state
adults.option_d: God hears all prayers but not all are answered
adults.explanation: Psalm 66:18 teaches that harboured sin (iniquity regarded in the heart) prevents God from hearing prayer — heart purity is prerequisite to answered prayer.

adults.question: What does the personal testimony section of Psalm 66:16-19 ("Come and hear, all ye that fear God") model for OT believers?
adults.option_b: Public declaration of God's personal acts of salvation — the testimony as worship and witness
adults.option_c: A priestly proclamation from the temple
adults.option_d: A legal deposition before the assembly
adults.explanation: Psalm 66:16-19 models the public testimony as an act of worship — "I will declare what he hath done for my soul" (Psalm 66:16) — personal faith shared communally.

adults.question: How does Psalm 66 transition between communal history (66:1-12) and personal testimony (66:13-20)?
adults.option_a: The speaker changes from a choir to a king
adults.option_b: The psalm moves from corporate salvation history to individual response and vow-fulfillment, integrating national and personal faith
adults.option_c: The two sections are unrelated fragments
adults.option_d: A liturgical rubric marks the division
adults.explanation: Psalm 66 is a masterful integration of corporate history (66:1-12) with personal testimony (66:13-20), showing that Israel's salvation story is also each believer's story.

kids.question: What did the psalmist ask God to do to his face in Psalm 67?
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "God be merciful unto us, and cause his face to shine upon us" (Psalm 67:1).

kids.question: What repeated phrase shows the psalmist's desire for all people to praise God?
kids.option_b: Let the people praise thee, O God; let all the people praise thee
kids.option_d: Bless the LORD all his works
kids.explanation: The refrain "Let the people praise thee, O God; let all the people praise thee" appears in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5.

kids.question: What does Psalm 67 say God shall do to show he is their God?
kids.option_b: Bless them and make the earth yield her increase
kids.option_c: Speak to them from a cloud
kids.explanation: The psalmist wrote, "Then shall the earth yield her increase; and God, even our own God, shall bless us" (Psalm 67:6).

teens.question: What is the purpose stated in Psalm 67:2 for God blessing Israel?
teens.option_a: That Israel would be the greatest nation
teens.option_b: That God's way may be known upon earth and his saving health among all nations
teens.option_c: That other nations would fear Israel
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed for God's blessing so "that thy way may be known upon earth, thy saving health among all nations" (Psalm 67:2).

teens.question: What reason is given for the nations to be glad in Psalm 67:4?
teens.option_b: God judges the people righteously and governs the nations upon earth
teens.option_c: God delivers them from their enemies
teens.option_d: God gives them wisdom and law
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "O let the nations be glad and sing for joy: for thou shalt judge the people righteously, and govern the nations upon earth" (Psalm 67:4).

teens.question: Psalm 67 is considered a "missionary psalm." What makes it missionary in character?
teens.option_a: It commands Israel to go to other nations
teens.option_b: It prays that God's salvation and blessing of Israel will become known to and shared with all nations
teens.option_d: It lists nations that will be converted
teens.explanation: Psalm 67 is missionary because it prays that Israel's blessing will reveal God's way to all nations (Psalm 67:2) — a centripetal mission theology.

teens.question: The opening of Psalm 67:1 echoes what famous OT blessing?
teens.option_c: The blessing of Jacob in Genesis 49
teens.option_d: The benediction at the end of Leviticus
teens.explanation: Psalm 67:1 echoes the Aaronic blessing: "The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee" (Numbers 6:25).

teens.question: How many times does the refrain "let the people praise thee" appear in Psalm 67?
teens.explanation: The refrain "Let the people praise thee, O God; let all the people praise thee" appears twice — in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5.

adults.question: How does Psalm 67's opening allusion to the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26) connect Israel's priestly vocation to missionary purpose?
adults.option_a: Israel is to be a nation that keeps blessings to itself
adults.option_b: Israel as a "kingdom of priests" (Exodus 19:6) receives God's blessing in order to mediate it to all nations
adults.option_c: The Aaronic blessing was only for the tribe of Levi
adults.option_d: The psalm rejects the Aaronic blessing as insufficient
adults.explanation: By echoing Numbers 6:24-26, Psalm 67 frames Israel's blessing within its priestly calling (Exodus 19:6) — blessed to be a blessing to all nations (cf. Genesis 12:3).

adults.question: What does "thy saving health" (Psalm 67:2) — literally "thy salvation" (yeshuah) — anticipate in redemptive history?
adults.option_a: The military victories of Israel's kings
adults.option_b: The universal proclamation of salvation through Yeshua (Jesus) to all nations
adults.option_c: The physical healing of all nations
adults.option_d: The agricultural prosperity described in verse 6
adults.explanation: The Hebrew yeshuah (saving health/salvation, Psalm 67:2) shares a root with Yeshua (Jesus) — pointing forward to the universal salvation Christ brings to all nations.

adults.question: What is the structural role of the refrain in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5?
adults.option_a: It marks liturgical pauses for the congregation to kneel
adults.option_b: It creates a concentric structure framing the reasons for universal praise — God's just rule (67:4) and his provision (67:6)
adults.option_c: It was sung by a different choir
adults.option_d: It divides the psalm into unrelated sections
adults.explanation: The refrain in Psalm 67:3 and 67:5 frames the central verse (67:4) about God's just governance of nations, creating a chiastic structure for the psalm.

adults.question: What two aspects of God's governance are highlighted in Psalm 67:4 as reasons for the nations to rejoice?
adults.option_b: Righteous judgment of peoples and just governing of nations
adults.option_c: His mercy and his patience
adults.option_d: His creation and his providence
adults.explanation: Psalm 67:4 says the nations should rejoice because God "shalt judge the people righteously, and govern the nations upon earth" — just rule is cause for universal joy.

adults.question: How does the agricultural blessing of Psalm 67:6 function within the psalm's theological argument?
adults.option_a: It is merely a prayer for good harvests
adults.option_b: It connects God's material provision of Israel to the missionary goal — visible blessing testifies to God's character before the nations
adults.option_c: It replaces the need for the temple
adults.option_d: It is a covenant condition for Israel's obedience
adults.explanation: The earth's yield (Psalm 67:6) is not isolated — Israel's visible blessing by God testifies to his character and draws nations to him (cf. Deuteronomy 28:1-10).

adults.question: What does "God, even our own God" (Psalm 67:6) emphasize in the context of a psalm addressed to all nations?
adults.option_a: God belongs exclusively to Israel and not the nations
adults.option_b: The universal God who blesses all nations is also the personal covenant God of Israel — particularity within universality
adults.option_c: God has different identities for different peoples
adults.option_d: The nations have their own gods and Israel has hers
adults.explanation: The juxtaposition of "all nations" (Psalm 67:2) with "our own God" (Psalm 67:6) holds together God's universal sovereignty and his particular covenant relationship with Israel.

adults.question: How does Psalm 67 anticipate the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19)?
adults.option_a: It commands Israel to go to all nations with the Torah
adults.option_b: It prays for all nations to know God's salvation — the same missional goal as Christ's command to make disciples of all nations
adults.option_c: It predicts that Israel will rule all nations
adults.option_d: It has no connection to the New Testament
adults.explanation: Psalm 67's prayer for all nations to know God's saving way (Psalm 67:2) is the OT anticipation of the Great Commission — the same universal missional heart.

adults.question: What is the logical flow of Psalm 67's theology: blessing → knowledge → praise → more blessing?
adults.option_a: God blesses Israel, all nations see it, all nations praise God, and God blesses all the earth
adults.option_b: God punishes nations, they repent, they praise, and God blesses them
adults.option_c: Israel praises God, God blesses them, the nations learn from Israel, and God judges the nations
adults.option_d: Nations praise God first, then he blesses them, then Israel sees his power
adults.explanation: Psalm 67's logic is: God blesses Israel (67:1) → his way known among nations (67:2) → nations praise (67:3-5) → earth yields increase and all are blessed (67:6-7).

kids.question: What does Psalm 68:1 say will happen to God's enemies when God arises?
kids.option_d: They will flee to other lands
kids.explanation: David wrote, "Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered: let them also that hate him flee before him" (Psalm 68:1).

kids.question: What does Psalm 68:5 call God in relation to those who have no father?
kids.explanation: David declared that God is "A father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows, is God in his holy habitation" (Psalm 68:5).

kids.question: What did Psalm 68:18 say God did on high — something Paul quotes in Ephesians 4:8?
kids.option_a: He built a throne in heaven
kids.option_b: He ascended on high and led captivity captive
kids.explanation: David wrote, "Thou hast ascended on high, thou hast led captivity captive: thou hast received gifts for men" (Psalm 68:18).

teens.question: What does Psalm 68 say God does for the solitary and the prisoners?
teens.option_a: He gives them strength to endure
teens.option_b: He sets the solitary in families and brings out prisoners into prosperity
teens.option_c: He sends angels to comfort them
teens.explanation: David wrote, "God setteth the solitary in families: he bringeth out those which are bound with chains" (Psalm 68:6).

teens.question: What natural phenomena accompanied God's march through the wilderness in Psalm 68?
teens.option_b: The earth shook and the heavens dropped rain
teens.explanation: David recalled, "The earth shook, the heavens also dropped at the presence of God: even Sinai itself was moved at the presence of God" (Psalm 68:8).

teens.question: How does Psalm 68:11 describe those who spread the good news of God's victory?
teens.option_b: As a great company of those that published it
teens.option_d: As prophets sent to the nations
teens.explanation: David wrote, "The Lord gave the word: great was the company of those that published it" (Psalm 68:11).

teens.question: What mountain does Psalm 68:16 identify as the mountain God desires to dwell in?
teens.explanation: David wrote, "This is the hill which God desireth to dwell in; yea, the LORD will dwell in it for ever" (Psalm 68:16) — referring to Zion.

teens.question: What does Psalm 68:20 say about God and the issues of death?
teens.option_a: Death is a punishment for the wicked only
teens.option_b: Unto God the Lord belong the issues from death
teens.option_c: God removes death from those who trust him
teens.option_d: Death comes only to those who rebel against God
teens.explanation: David declared, "Unto God the Lord belong the issues from death" (Psalm 68:20) — God controls the gates of death.

adults.question: How does Psalm 68:1 echo Numbers 10:35, and what is the theological significance of this echo?
adults.option_a: Both describe the ark of the covenant in battle
adults.option_b: Both use the priestly battle cry when the ark advanced, connecting Israel's worship march to God's warrior presence
adults.option_c: Both are thanksgiving psalms for military victory
adults.option_d: Both describe the return of the ark to Jerusalem
adults.explanation: Psalm 68:1 directly quotes Numbers 10:35 — "Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered" — the ancient ark-march formula, situating Israel's worship in God's warrior-presence.

adults.question: Paul quotes Psalm 68:18 in Ephesians 4:8, applying it to Christ's ascension. What does "led captivity captive" mean in both the OT and NT contexts?
adults.option_a: Both refer to taking prisoners of war as slaves
adults.option_b: In the OT it describes God's triumph over enemies; in the NT Paul applies it to Christ's victory over sin, death, and spiritual powers at his ascension
adults.option_c: The verse only applies to Pentecost
adults.option_d: Paul misquotes the verse by changing "received" to "gave"
adults.explanation: Psalm 68:18's triumphal ascent (God leading captives) is applied by Paul (Ephesians 4:8) to Christ's ascension, which conquered spiritual enemies and distributed gifts to the church.

adults.question: What does the description of God as "father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows" (Psalm 68:5) reveal about Israel's covenant ethics?
adults.option_a: God only cares for Israel's military strength
adults.option_b: God's character demands care for the most vulnerable — the fatherless and widow are special objects of his concern
adults.option_c: Widows and orphans were excluded from worship
adults.option_d: This is merely a poetic exaggeration
