adults.explanation: God as father of the fatherless and defender of widows (Psalm 68:5) grounds Israel's social ethics in divine character — care for the vulnerable is a covenant obligation (cf. Deuteronomy 10:18).

adults.question: What is the significance of Mount Bashan's envy of Mount Zion in Psalm 68:15-16?
adults.option_a: Bashan was physically taller than Zion
adults.option_b: High, imposing mountains are superseded by small Zion — God's choice of the humble place over the impressive demonstrates his sovereign freedom
adults.option_c: Bashan was Israel's enemy territory
adults.option_d: The psalm describes a literal geological event
adults.explanation: The contrast between high Bashan and small Zion (Psalm 68:15-16) shows that God chooses the lowly — his dwelling is not determined by natural grandeur but by his sovereign will.

adults.question: What is the eschatological scope of Psalm 68:29-32, which anticipates kings bringing gifts to God?
adults.option_a: It is limited to Solomon's temple dedications
adults.option_b: It anticipates the eschatological ingathering of nations to Zion — a Gentile submission to God's kingship
adults.option_c: It describes the tribute of conquered enemies
adults.option_d: It refers to the return from Babylonian exile
adults.explanation: Psalm 68:29-32 anticipates kings from Egypt and Ethiopia bringing gifts to God (cf. Isaiah 60) — an eschatological vision of universal Gentile submission to Israel's God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 68's procession theology relate to the physical movement of the ark of the covenant?
adults.option_a: It is purely metaphorical and unrelated to the ark
adults.option_b: The psalm likely accompanied the ark's triumphal procession to Jerusalem, making God's physical march with his people the occasion for cosmic theology
adults.option_c: The ark is never mentioned in the psalm
adults.option_d: The psalm was written centuries after the ark was lost
adults.explanation: Psalm 68 is widely understood as a liturgical processional accompanying the ark — God's physical march with Israel (68:7-8) becomes the lens for cosmic theology.

adults.question: What does the list of tribes in Psalm 68:27 (Benjamin, Judah, Zebulun, Naphtali) represent?
adults.option_a: The four tribes that led Israel's armies
adults.option_b: A representative gathering of all Israel — north and south, small and great — united in worship
adults.option_c: The tribes that accompanied the ark from the wilderness
adults.option_d: The four cardinal points of Israel's tribal arrangement
adults.explanation: The four tribes (Psalm 68:27) represent all Israel — Benjamin and Judah from the south, Zebulun and Naphtali from the north — the whole nation united before God.

adults.question: What is the theological climax of Psalm 68, and where does it occur?
adults.option_a: The call to praise in verse 1
adults.option_b: The universal praise of all kingdoms and nations in verses 32-35, culminating in the declaration of God's strength and power
adults.option_c: The ascension of God in verse 18
adults.option_d: The defeat of enemies in verse 21
adults.explanation: Psalm 68 climaxes in a universal call for all kingdoms to sing to God (Psalm 68:32-35) — all the earth declaring his strength and power above all.

kids.question: What did the enemies give David to drink in Psalm 69?
kids.explanation: David wrote, "They gave me also gall for my meat; and in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink" (Psalm 69:21).

kids.question: What did David say consumed him regarding God's house?
kids.option_a: A great love for its beauty
kids.option_c: A desire to serve as a priest
kids.option_d: A longing to see God's glory
kids.explanation: David said, "The zeal of thine house hath eaten me up" (Psalm 69:9).

kids.question: To what was David sinking in the opening of Psalm 69?
kids.explanation: David cried, "I sink in deep mire, where there is no standing: I am come into deep waters, where the floods overflow me" (Psalm 69:2).

teens.question: What does Psalm 69:9 say about the reproaches of those who reproached God?
teens.explanation: David wrote, "The reproaches of them that reproached thee are fallen upon me" (Psalm 69:9).

teens.question: What was David's condition that made him a stranger to his family in Psalm 69:8?
teens.option_b: His zeal for God made him an alien to his brethren and a stranger to his mother's children
teens.option_c: His long absence on a journey
teens.explanation: David wrote, "I am become a stranger unto my brethren, and an alien unto my mother's children" because of his zeal for God (Psalm 69:8-9).

teens.question: What did David say his enemies hated him without a cause for in Psalm 69:4?
teens.option_b: They that hate me without a cause are more than the hairs of mine head
teens.explanation: David lamented, "They that hate me without a cause are more than the hairs of mine head" (Psalm 69:4).

teens.question: How does Psalm 69:21 connect to the crucifixion of Jesus?
teens.option_a: Jesus quoted this verse on the cross
teens.option_b: The vinegar given to Jesus at the cross (Matthew 27:48; John 19:29) fulfills this verse
teens.option_c: Jesus prayed this psalm in Gethsemane
teens.option_d: John cites it as fulfilled at the Last Supper
teens.explanation: Psalm 69:21 — "in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink" — is fulfilled in John 19:29 when soldiers offered Jesus vinegar (sour wine) at the crucifixion.

teens.question: What does the psalmist say God will do for the humble and the poor in the closing section of Psalm 69:32-33?
teens.option_b: The humble shall see and be glad; the LORD heareth the poor
teens.explanation: David declared, "The humble shall see this, and be glad... For the LORD heareth the poor, and despiseth not his prisoners" (Psalm 69:32-33).

adults.question: How many times is Psalm 69 quoted or alluded to in the New Testament, making it one of the most-cited OT texts?
adults.option_b: About three times
adults.option_c: At least seven times
adults.option_d: Once
adults.explanation: Psalm 69 is one of the most-quoted psalms in the NT — cited at least seven times including Romans 11:9-10, John 2:17, John 15:25, Matthew 27:48, Acts 1:20, and Romans 15:3.

adults.question: John 2:17 quotes Psalm 69:9 in reference to Jesus cleansing the temple. What theological connection does John draw?
adults.option_a: Jesus was fulfilling a legal obligation
adults.option_b: Jesus' zeal for God's house consuming him identifies him as the messianic sufferer of Psalm 69 who bears the cost of God's honour
adults.option_c: Jesus was angry at the merchants personally
adults.option_d: John uses the verse to show Jesus loved the temple building
adults.explanation: John 2:17 applies Psalm 69:9 to Jesus — his consuming zeal for God's house identifies him as the messianic servant who bears divine reproach (John 2:17; cf. Romans 15:3).

adults.question: How does "they hated me without a cause" (Psalm 69:4) function in John 15:25?
adults.option_a: Jesus quotes it as a legal precedent
adults.option_b: Jesus applies it to himself — the world's hatred of him without cause fulfills Scripture, confirming his messianic identity
adults.option_c: John uses it as a general proverb
adults.option_d: It is used to defend the disciples' innocence
adults.explanation: Jesus directly applies Psalm 69:4 in John 15:25 — the world's baseless hatred of him "fulfilleth the word that is written in their law" — messianic fulfillment.

adults.question: What is the imprecatory nature of Psalm 69:22-28, and how has it been applied in the NT?
adults.option_a: It is a prayer for enemies' salvation
adults.option_b: It calls God's judgment on enemies; Acts 1:20 applies verse 25 to Judas Iscariot's fate
adults.option_c: It is purely historical and never applied in the NT
adults.option_d: It predicts the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD
adults.explanation: Psalm 69:25 ("Let their habitation be desolate") is quoted in Acts 1:20 as fulfilled in Judas Iscariot, whose betrayal brought upon him the judgment prophesied here.

adults.question: What does the opening water imagery (sinking in mire, floods, deep waters, Psalm 69:1-3, 14-15) represent as a metaphorical system?
adults.option_b: Chaotic, overwhelming suffering threatening to extinguish life — the deep waters symbolize mortal crisis beyond human rescue
adults.option_c: The waters of the Red Sea
adults.option_d: A spiritual purification ritual
adults.explanation: The deep waters and mire of Psalm 69:1-3, 14-15 are ancient Near Eastern chaos imagery — overwhelming, life-threatening suffering from which only God can save.

adults.question: What does "blot them out of the book of the living" (Psalm 69:28) imply about Israel's theology of divine records?
adults.option_a: It is merely a poetic wish
adults.option_b: It implies a heavenly register of the living from which the condemned are erased — anticipating the book of life in Revelation
adults.option_c: It refers to the genealogical records of Israel
adults.option_d: It is a priestly curse formula
adults.explanation: Psalm 69:28's book of the living anticipates the NT book of life (Revelation 3:5; 20:15) — a divine register of those in covenant relationship with God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 69:30-31 theologically reorder Israel's sacrificial system?
adults.option_b: It subordinates animal sacrifice to the praise of a thankful heart — God is more pleased with grateful song than burnt offerings
adults.option_c: It argues that sacrifices are more important than praise
adults.option_d: It restricts praise to the Levitical singers
adults.explanation: Psalm 69:30-31 declares that praising God with a song pleases him more than an ox or bullock — an OT anticipation of the prophetic critique of empty ritual (cf. Psalm 51:16-17; Hosea 6:6).

adults.question: What does Psalm 69's structure (lament → imprecation → praise → eschatological hope) model for NT believers facing unjust suffering?
adults.option_a: That imprecatory prayer is forbidden in the NT
adults.option_b: That honest lament can coexist with praise, and God's ultimate justice transforms present suffering into eschatological hope
adults.option_c: That believers should suppress their complaints
adults.option_d: That suffering is always the result of personal sin
adults.explanation: Psalm 69 models the journey from raw lament (69:1-4) through honest prayer (69:5-28) to confident praise (69:30-36) — a model for NT believers facing unjust suffering (cf. Romans 8:18-25).

kids.question: What did David urgently ask God to do in the very first verse of Psalm 70?
kids.option_c: Send his angels to protect him
kids.explanation: David prayed, "Make haste, O God, to deliver me; make haste to help me, O LORD" (Psalm 70:1).

kids.question: What did David ask God to do to those who sought his life?
kids.option_b: Let them be ashamed and confounded
kids.option_c: Send them away to other lands
kids.option_d: Give them a chance to repent
kids.explanation: David prayed, "Let them be ashamed and confounded that seek after my soul" (Psalm 70:2).

kids.question: What did David call himself — showing his need for God?
kids.explanation: David concluded, "But I am poor and needy: make haste unto me, O God" (Psalm 70:5).

teens.question: What does David say the seekers of God should continually say?
teens.option_a: Bless the LORD all ye peoples
teens.option_c: Praise the LORD for he is good
teens.option_d: Holy is the LORD God Almighty
teens.explanation: David said, "Let all those that seek thee rejoice and be glad in thee: and let such as love thy salvation say continually, Let God be magnified" (Psalm 70:4).

teens.question: What does David pray about those who say "Aha, aha!" (mocking him)?
teens.option_b: That they will be turned backward and put to shame
teens.option_d: That they will be defeated in battle
teens.explanation: David prayed, "Let them be turned backward, and put to confusion, that desire my hurt... that say, Aha, aha" (Psalm 70:2-3).

teens.question: Psalm 70 is almost identical to which earlier psalm?
teens.explanation: Psalm 70 is nearly identical to Psalm 40:13-17 — both are urgent prayers for deliverance, with Psalm 70 being an independent liturgical use of that section.

teens.question: What does the brevity of Psalm 70 (only 5 verses) communicate about the nature of urgent prayer?
teens.option_b: Genuine crisis prayer can be intense and brief — urgency strips prayer to its essentials
teens.option_c: David was too tired to write more
teens.explanation: Psalm 70's five-verse brevity captures the essence of crisis prayer — urgency and desperate need strip the prayer down to its bare essentials before God.

teens.question: What superscription or title does Psalm 70 carry that indicates its liturgical function?
teens.option_b: A psalm of David to bring to remembrance
teens.explanation: Psalm 70 is headed "A Psalm of David, to bring to remembrance" — suggesting it was used as a memorial/petition in Israel's liturgy.

adults.question: What does "to bring to remembrance" in Psalm 70's superscription suggest about its liturgical use?
adults.option_a: David wanted future generations to memorize it
adults.option_b: It was used in the context of the "memorial offering" (azkarah) — a ritual bringing the worshipper's need before God's attention
adults.option_c: It was a royal decree
adults.option_d: It was placed in the temple archives
adults.explanation: The title "to bring to remembrance" (Psalm 70 superscription) likely connects to the azkarah (memorial portion of the grain offering) — a liturgical bringing of oneself before God.

adults.question: How does Psalm 70's repetition of "make haste" (verses 1 and 5) function rhetorically?
adults.option_a: It shows David's impatience with God
adults.option_b: The double urgency frames the entire psalm — every word is under the pressure of desperate need
adults.option_c: It is a liturgical chorus
adults.option_d: It balances the two stanzas
adults.explanation: The twin urgency of "make haste" in Psalm 70:1 and 70:5 creates an envelope of crisis — the whole psalm breathes urgency and radical dependence on God.

adults.question: What does the contrast between the fate of enemies (ashamed, confounded, 70:2-3) and the righteous (glad, rejoicing, 70:4) reveal about OT theodicy?
adults.option_a: Righteous and wicked have identical destinies
adults.option_b: God's justice guarantees opposite outcomes — the wicked's shame is the necessary counterpart to the righteous's joy
adults.option_c: Righteousness guarantees immediate reward
adults.option_d: The wicked are not judged until death
adults.explanation: Psalm 70's sharp contrast — enemies confounded (70:2-3) vs. seekers rejoicing (70:4) — reflects OT theodicy: God's justice ensures that righteousness and wickedness lead to opposite ends.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of David's self-description as "poor and needy" (Psalm 70:5) for a king?
adults.option_a: David was literally impoverished at this time
adults.option_b: It expresses radical spiritual poverty — total dependence on God regardless of earthly status, the posture of the "anawim" (humble poor)
adults.option_c: It was a rhetorical device to gain sympathy
adults.option_d: It describes David's early pre-royal life
adults.explanation: David calling himself poor and needy (Psalm 70:5) despite being king reflects the anawim spirituality — the humble poor who depend entirely on God (cf. Matthew 5:3, "poor in spirit").

adults.question: When Psalm 70 is compared to Psalm 40:13-17, what does the independent use of this prayer section reveal?
adults.option_a: One version is a scribal error
adults.option_b: Individual psalm sections circulated independently and were reused in different liturgical contexts — scripture is organically living
adults.option_c: Psalm 70 corrects an error in Psalm 40
adults.option_d: David wrote Psalm 40 first and regretted it
adults.explanation: The existence of Psalm 70 as a standalone version of Psalm 40:13-17 shows that psalm sections were excerpted and reused liturgically — Israel's prayer treasury was fluid and living.

adults.question: How does "let God be magnified" (Psalm 70:4) function as a statement of priority within urgent crisis prayer?
adults.option_a: It delays God's help by praising first
adults.option_b: Even in desperate crisis, David's ultimate concern is God's magnification — the worshipper's need is subordinated to God's glory
adults.option_c: It is a standard liturgical formula
adults.option_d: It means David wants God to appear visibly
adults.explanation: David's cry "let God be magnified" (Psalm 70:4) within an urgent lament reveals that even crisis prayer is ultimately theocentric — God's glory is the supreme concern.

adults.question: What does the use of "LORD" (YHWH) in Psalm 70:1 and "God" (Elohim) in Psalm 70:1 and 5 reveal about the psalm's divine address?
adults.option_a: David is uncertain which name to use
adults.option_b: Both the personal covenant name (YHWH) and the generic divine name (Elohim) are used, appealing to God in both his covenant relationship and universal power
adults.option_c: The names are interchangeable with no significance
adults.option_d: YHWH is used for praise and Elohim for petition
adults.explanation: Psalm 70 addresses both YHWH (covenant name, 70:1) and Elohim (universal name, 70:1, 5), appealing to both God's personal covenant faithfulness and his omnipotent power.

adults.question: How does Psalm 70's raw urgency contribute to the Psalter's honest theology of prayer?
adults.option_b: It validates desperate, unadorned cries to God — the Psalter permits and models raw urgency as legitimate prayer
adults.option_c: It shows that only emotional prayers are answered
adults.option_d: It warns against praying too urgently
adults.explanation: Psalm 70's unadorned urgency contributes to the Psalter's honest prayer theology — it legitimises stripped-down crisis prayer as fully valid before God (cf. Romans 8:26).

kids.question: What did the psalmist ask God not to do in his old age in Psalm 71?
kids.option_b: Cast him off in the time of old age
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "Cast me not off in the time of old age; forsake me not when my strength faileth" (Psalm 71:9).

kids.question: What did the psalmist say he had leaned on since his birth?
kids.option_b: God who took him out of his mother's womb
kids.option_c: The strength of his own body
kids.explanation: The psalmist said, "By thee have I been holden up from the womb: thou art he that took me out of my mother's bowels" (Psalm 71:6).

kids.question: What did the psalmist say he would always hope in and praise God more and more?
kids.explanation: The psalmist declared, "But I will hope continually, and will yet praise thee more and more" (Psalm 71:14).

teens.question: What did the psalmist's enemies say when they plotted against him in Psalm 71:11?
teens.option_a: God has given him to us
teens.option_b: God hath forsaken him: persecute and take him; for there is none to deliver him
teens.option_c: He has no friends to help him
teens.option_d: His God will not hear his prayer
teens.explanation: The psalmist's enemies said, "God hath forsaken him: persecute and take him; for there is none to deliver him" (Psalm 71:11).

teens.question: What does the psalmist say he will shew to this generation and everyone to come?
teens.explanation: The psalmist declared, "I will shew thy strength unto this generation, and thy power to every one that is to come" (Psalm 71:18).

teens.question: What musical instrument does the psalmist mention praising God with in Psalm 71?
teens.explanation: The psalmist declared, "I will also praise thee with the psaltery, even thy truth, O my God: unto thee will I sing with the harp" (Psalm 71:22).

teens.question: What does the psalmist ask God to do after showing him troubles and adversities in Psalm 71?
teens.option_b: Quicken him again and bring him up again from the depths of the earth
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "Thou, which hast shewed me great and sore troubles, shalt quicken me again, and shalt bring me up again from the depths of the earth" (Psalm 71:20).

teens.question: How does the psalmist describe God as his refuge from his youth in Psalm 71:5?
teens.option_a: As his daily bread and water
teens.option_b: As his hope and his trust from his youth
teens.option_c: As the guide of all his ways
teens.option_d: As the teacher of his people
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "For thou art my hope, O Lord GOD: thou art my trust from my youth" (Psalm 71:5).

adults.question: What is the likely setting of Psalm 71 and what makes it distinctive among the Psalms?
adults.option_a: A young warrior going into battle
adults.option_b: It is the prayer of an elderly believer — the only psalm explicitly voiced from the perspective of old age and lifelong faith
adults.option_c: A king celebrating his coronation
adults.option_d: A priest performing his annual duties
adults.explanation: Psalm 71 is unique as the prayer of an aged person (Psalm 71:9, 18) — a lifelong believer reviewing God's faithfulness from birth to old age.

adults.question: What does the psalmist's phrase "thou art my strong refuge" (Psalm 71:7) — using the Hebrew "mahseh" — convey about his theology?
adults.option_a: God is physically protective like a wall
adults.option_b: Mahseh (refuge/shelter) describes God as a place of total security — the aged believer's whole life has been spent under divine shelter
adults.option_c: God rescued the psalmist from a specific battle
adults.option_d: God is the refuge only in times of crisis
adults.explanation: Mahseh (Psalm 71:7) is a rich refuge word — not just emergency protection but a lifelong dwelling in God's sheltering care from womb to old age.

adults.question: How does Psalm 71's interlacing of petition and praise (e.g., 71:1-4 petition, 71:5-8 praise, 71:9-13 petition) model the rhythm of mature prayer?
adults.option_a: It shows that the psalmist forgot his train of thought
adults.option_b: It models prayer as a living dialogue where trust and need alternate — praise does not wait until deliverance but interrupts lament
adults.option_c: The sections were originally separate prayers
adults.option_d: Petition and praise are kept strictly separate
adults.explanation: Psalm 71's interweaving of petition and praise models mature prayer — praise interrupts lament (71:5-8) because faith does not wait for deliverance to worship God.

adults.question: What does "I will go in the strength of the Lord GOD" (Psalm 71:16) declare about the aged believer's source of ongoing vitality?
adults.option_a: Physical strength is restored to the aged
adults.option_b: In the absence of youthful strength, God's strength becomes the sole source of the aged believer's forward movement
adults.option_c: The psalmist is beginning a military campaign
adults.option_d: God gives the aged special supernatural ability
adults.explanation: Psalm 71:16's "I will go in the strength of the Lord GOD" is a declaration of radical dependence — the aged believer's vitality comes entirely from God, not from self.

adults.question: What does the psalmist's request in Psalm 71:18 — "forsake me not...until I have shewed thy strength unto this generation" — reveal about his understanding of his life's purpose in old age?
adults.option_a: Old age has no remaining purpose
adults.option_b: The aged believer's final purpose is testimony — to pass the knowledge of God's power to the next generation
adults.option_c: The psalmist fears death before completing temple service
adults.option_d: God abandons the elderly when their strength fails
adults.explanation: Psalm 71:18's request to live until he can testify to the next generation reveals that the aged believer's final vocation is intergenerational witness — faith passed to the next generation.

adults.question: How does the psalmist's reference to being held from the womb (71:6) relate to his trust in God in old age?
adults.option_b: It creates a theological arc: if God was faithful from birth, he will be faithful in old age — the whole life as evidence for continued trust
adults.option_c: Birth and old age are unrelated in the psalm
adults.option_d: It refers to a miraculous birth like Isaac or Samuel
adults.explanation: Psalm 71's womb-to-old-age arc (71:6, 9, 18) builds a cumulative argument: God's faithfulness across an entire life is the basis for trust in life's final chapter.

adults.question: What is the significance of the psalmist's vow to praise God with "lips that rejoice" (Psalm 71:23) when God restores him?
adults.option_b: The lips that cried in lament will become the lips of praise — suffering and praise are part of one continuous testimony
adults.option_c: Praise is only appropriate after complete victory
adults.option_d: The psalmist had previously been silenced
adults.explanation: Psalm 71:23's rejoicing lips complete the journey from the crying soul of 71:2-3 — the same mouth that begged becomes the mouth that praises, unifying lament and doxology.

adults.question: How does Psalm 71 relate to the broader wisdom tradition's teaching on the value of old age?
adults.option_a: Old age is seen as a burden without spiritual value
adults.option_b: It affirms the wisdom tradition's view (Proverbs 16:31; 20:29) that old age can be a crown of lifelong faithfulness — a platform for testimony
adults.option_c: The psalm is pessimistic about aging
adults.option_d: Psalm 71 contradicts Proverbs on old age
adults.explanation: Psalm 71 aligns with wisdom tradition (Proverbs 16:31 — "hoary head is a crown of glory") by presenting old age as the culmination of faithful life and a platform for testimony.

kids.question: How far will the king's dominion reach according to Psalm 72?
kids.option_b: From sea to sea and to the ends of the earth
kids.option_d: From the north to the south only
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "He shall have dominion also from sea to sea, and from the river unto the ends of the earth" (Psalm 72:8).

kids.question: What does the psalmist say the king will do for the poor and needy?
kids.option_b: Save the children of the needy and break in pieces the oppressor
kids.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "He shall save the children of the needy, and shall break in pieces the oppressor" (Psalm 72:4).

kids.question: What is said about the king's name in Psalm 72?
kids.option_a: It shall be feared by all nations
kids.option_b: His name shall endure for ever
kids.option_c: It shall be written in the heavens
kids.option_d: All kings shall know his name
kids.explanation: The psalmist declared, "His name shall endure for ever: his name shall be continued as long as the sun" (Psalm 72:17).

teens.question: What two things does the king judge righteously in Psalm 72?
teens.option_b: The poor and the children of the needy
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed that the king would "judge thy people with righteousness, and thy poor with judgment" (Psalm 72:2) and deliver the needy.

teens.question: What natural image illustrates the king's refreshing rule over the people in Psalm 72:6?
teens.option_a: A mighty wind that cools the land
teens.option_b: He shall come down like rain upon the mown grass
teens.option_c: A river of water in a dry land
teens.option_d: A spring that never runs dry
teens.explanation: The psalmist said, "He shall come down like rain upon the mown grass: as showers that water the earth" (Psalm 72:6).

teens.question: Who does Psalm 72 say shall bow before the king — specifically mentioning nations from the south?
teens.option_a: The kings of the north only
teens.option_b: The kings of Sheba and Seba shall offer gifts, all kings shall fall down before him
teens.option_d: The people of Egypt and Babylon
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "The kings of Sheba and Seba shall offer gifts... all kings shall fall down before him: all nations shall serve him" (Psalm 72:10-11).

teens.question: What phrase closes Book II of the Psalms (Psalms 42-72) at the end of Psalm 72?
teens.option_b: The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended
teens.option_c: Let all the earth be filled with his glory
teens.option_d: Praise ye the LORD for ever and ever
teens.explanation: Psalm 72:20 reads, "The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended" — the editorial colophon closing Book II of the Psalter.

teens.question: What does the psalmist say the king will do to the man in need who has no helper in Psalm 72:12?
teens.option_a: Give him a judge to plead his case
teens.option_b: Deliver him; he shall spare the poor and needy
teens.option_c: Send him to the priests for help
teens.option_d: Give him a field to work
teens.explanation: The psalmist prayed, "He shall deliver the needy when he crieth; the poor also, and him that hath no helper" (Psalm 72:12).

adults.question: What is the dual horizon of Psalm 72 — which two kings does it simultaneously address?
adults.option_b: Solomon historically and the Messiah eschatologically — it is both a royal coronation prayer and a Messianic prophecy
adults.option_c: David and Moses
adults.option_d: Solomon and Hezekiah
adults.explanation: Psalm 72 is simultaneously a prayer for Solomon at his coronation (its historical horizon) and a Messianic prophecy (its eschatological horizon) — no earthly king fully fulfills its vision.

adults.question: How does "he shall have dominion from sea to sea" (Psalm 72:8) connect to the Abrahamic covenant and its land promises?
adults.option_a: It refers to Israel's conquest of Canaan
adults.option_b: It expands the land promise beyond Canaan to universal sovereignty — the king is the heir of all God's covenant promises to Abraham
adults.option_c: It describes the borders of Solomon's kingdom
adults.option_d: It is merely poetic hyperbole
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:8's universal dominion echoes the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 22:17-18) that Abraham's seed would possess the gates of enemies — the king is the covenant heir.

adults.question: What does the abundance-of-grain image (Psalm 72:16 — "handful of corn on top of mountains") convey about the ideal king's reign?
adults.option_a: Israel will become an agricultural superpower
adults.option_b: Messianic rule brings abundant, impossible fruitfulness — even barren mountaintops overflow with grain, reversing the curse
adults.option_c: The king will be known for farming innovations
adults.option_d: The mountains will be terraced for agriculture
adults.explanation: Grain on mountain tops (Psalm 72:16) is a hyperbole of impossible abundance — Messianic rule reverses the curse and creates superabundant shalom (cf. Amos 9:13).

adults.question: How does Paul connect the universal blessing through the king's name (Psalm 72:17 — "all nations shall be blessed in him") to the Abrahamic covenant in Galatians 3?
adults.option_a: Paul does not connect these texts
adults.option_b: Psalm 72:17 echoes Genesis 12:3 ("all families of the earth shall be blessed") — the Messianic king is the Seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed
adults.option_c: The blessing is limited to nations that accept the Torah
adults.option_d: The blessing refers to material prosperity only
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:17's "all nations shall be blessed in him" directly echoes Genesis 12:3 — the Messianic king is identified as the Abrahamic Seed through whom universal blessing comes (cf. Galatians 3:16).

adults.question: What is the ethical centre of Psalm 72's vision of ideal kingship?
adults.option_b: Justice for the poor, needy, and oppressed — the king's greatness is measured by his care for the vulnerable
adults.option_c: Wisdom and learning above all virtues
adults.option_d: Wealth and international tribute
adults.explanation: The ethical centre of Psalm 72 is justice for the poor and oppressed (72:2, 4, 12-14) — the ideal king's greatness is measured not by conquest but by how he protects the vulnerable.

adults.question: What does "he shall live" (Psalm 72:15) in the context of the psalm's universalism suggest about the Messianic king's life?
adults.option_a: The king will simply have a long reign
adults.option_b: It anticipates the eternal life of the Messianic king whose reign has no end — fulfilled in Christ's resurrection
adults.option_c: It is a prayer for Solomon's health
adults.option_d: It means the king will survive his enemies
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:15's "he shall live" within the context of eternal dominion (72:5, 17) points beyond any mortal king to the Messiah whose life and reign are eternal.

adults.question: What is the doxological function of Psalm 72:18-19 within the Psalter?
adults.option_b: It is the closing doxology of Book II (Psalms 42-72), a liturgical benediction praising God whose glory fills the whole earth
adults.option_c: It introduces Book III
adults.option_d: It is a separate psalm misplaced here
adults.explanation: Psalm 72:18-19 is the closing doxology of Book II of the Psalter (Psalms 42-72), blessing God and praying for his glory to fill all the earth.

adults.question: How does the image of the king defending the poor (Psalm 72:12-14) — "their blood shall be precious in his sight" — anticipate Christ's redemptive work?
adults.option_a: It is limited to economic welfare programs
adults.option_b: The king who redeems the weak from oppression and counts their blood precious foreshadows Christ who redeems at the cost of his own precious blood
adults.option_c: It describes Solomon's judicial reforms
adults.option_d: It is a military metaphor for protecting citizens
adults.explanation: The king's redemption of the needy with precious blood (Psalm 72:14) foreshadows Christ whose own blood (1 Peter 1:19) is the price of redemption for the weak and helpless.
